turns.content: When should I use git rebase instead of merge? I keep hearing conflicting advice.

Rebase and merge both combine histories, but they do it in different ways. - git merge takes two branches and creates a new “merge commit” that has both histories as parents. It preserves the exact branching structure: you can see where the branch diverged and where it came back together. - git rebase takes the commits from one branch and “replays” them on top of another base commit, producing new commits with new hashes. It makes history look linear, as if you started your work from the latest base all along. When to use which depends on what you’re optimizing for: Use merge when: - You want to preserve the true historical record of branching (including parallel development). - You’re integrating a long-lived branch where the merge itself is meaningful. - You’re working with commits that are already published and you don’t want to rewrite them. Use rebase when: - You want a clean, linear history for your own feature branch before integrating it. - You want to update your branch on top of the latest main without introducing a merge commit. - You’re cleaning up local commits (reordering, squashing, dropping, editing) before sharing. The biggest rule: don’t rebase commits that other people (or other clones) already depend on, because rewriting them changes commit IDs and forces tricky reconciliation later.
I think I get the broad idea, but can you explain what “replaying commits” really means? Like what happens under the hood?

“Replaying commits” means Git takes each commit from your branch (that isn’t already in the target base) and applies its patch one-by-one onto the new base, creating new commits. Concretely, suppose you have: - main: A — B — C - feature: A — B — C — D — E Now main moves forward: - main: A — B — C — F — G - feature still: A — B — C — D — E If you merge main into feature (while on feature, run git merge main), you get: A — B — C — D — E — M \       / F — G M is a merge commit whose parents are E and G. If you rebase feature onto main (git rebase main while on feature), Git finds commits on feature not in main: D and E. It temporarily “unhooks” them, resets feature to G, then applies D’s diff onto G to make D’, then applies E’s diff onto D’ to make E’. Result: - main: A — B — C — F — G - feature: A — B — C — F — G — D’ — E’ D’ and E’ have new hashes because their parent changed (and commit hash depends on parent, tree, metadata). The content might be identical (or slightly different if conflicts got resolved during rebase). Under the hood, it’s basically: 1) Determine the fork point (common ancestor). 2) Compute the list of commits to transplant. 3) For each commit, apply its patch (like a 3-way merge) onto the new base. 4) Create a new commit object for each.
So rebasing changes commit hashes because the parent changes. Is that the only reason? What else affects the hash?

Parent change is a big reason, but more generally a commit hash changes if any part of the commit object changes. A Git commit object includes: - The tree hash (snapshot of the working directory state at that commit) - Parent commit hash(es) - Author name/email/timestamp - Committer name/email/timestamp - Commit message So during rebase: - Parent changes → commit hash changes even if the tree is identical. - Committer timestamp usually changes (because you’re creating a new commit now), which also changes the hash. - If you resolve conflicts differently, the resulting tree changes too. That’s why rebased commits are “new” commits: they are different objects.
People say “merge preserves history” and “rebase rewrites history.” But merge also changes history by adding a commit. Isn’t that rewriting too?

Good distinction. Both alter the repository’s history graph, but “rewriting history” in Git jargon typically means changing existing commits (replacing them with different commits), not merely adding new commits. - Merge adds a new commit (the merge commit) without changing any existing commit objects. Old commits keep the same hashes and remain reachable. - Rebase creates new commits to replace existing ones (D becomes D’, E becomes E’). The original commits still exist in the object database for a while, but your branch reference moves to point to the new chain, and the old commits may become unreachable and eventually garbage-collected. So merge is “history-preserving” in the sense that it keeps existing commits intact and records the integration as an explicit event. Rebase is “history-rewriting” because it replaces commits with new ones and changes the apparent sequence of development.
Can you show me a simple ASCII graph example where the difference is obvious? I always get lost.

Sure. Start: main:   A---B---C feature:         \---D---E Now main advances: main:   A---B---C---F---G feature:         \---D---E 1) Merge main into feature (on feature: git merge main): main:   A---B---C---F---G feature:         \---D---E---M \     / F---G More clearly with parents: D---E /     \ A---B---C---F---G \     / \---M M has two parents: E and G. 2) Rebase feature onto main (on feature: git rebase main): main:   A---B---C---F---G feature:                 \---D'---E' The branch “looks” linear, and D’, E’ are new commits.
Okay, so if I want a linear history, I use rebase. But why do people care so much about linear history? Isn’t the graph the real truth?

The graph is the real truth, but “truth” isn’t the only objective; readability and tooling matter. Reasons some people like linear history: - Easier to read with git log (without extra options). A linear sequence can be scanned top-down. - bisect can be conceptually simpler because you don’t have as many merge commits to interpret. - Some prefer each feature to appear as a contiguous block of commits on main (especially when merged via fast-forward). - It can make “what changed since tag X” feel cleaner. Reasons some people prefer merges (non-linear history): - Merge commits capture integration points explicitly: “these two lines of development were combined here.” - You can preserve context: e.g., a feature branch with multiple subtopics can remain a branch structure. - Conflict resolution can be stored in the merge commit once, instead of potentially re-resolving during rebases (though rerere can help). - It avoids rewriting commits that might have been referenced elsewhere. There’s no universal best choice. It’s about the kind of history you want to maintain and the workflows you prefer.
What’s a fast-forward merge and how does it relate to rebase?

A fast-forward (FF) merge happens when the target branch tip is an ancestor of the branch being merged. Then Git can just move the branch pointer forward—no merge commit needed. Example: main:   A---B---C feature:         \---D---E If main hasn’t moved since branching (main still at C), and you merge feature into main: main: A---B---C---D---E (feature pointer also at E) That’s a fast-forward merge: main pointer advances to E. How it relates to rebase: - Rebasing a feature branch onto the current main often makes it possible to do a fast-forward merge later, because your feature commits sit “on top of” main with no divergence. - If main has advanced, merging feature into main without rebase would typically create a merge commit (unless you use strategies like rebase-and-merge).
I’ve seen “rebase and merge” and “squash and merge” mentioned. How are those different from normal merge?

These are conceptual integration styles that many people implement manually with Git commands. 1) Normal merge (often “merge commit”): - You merge a branch into another and create a merge commit (unless fast-forward is possible and allowed). - Preserves branch topology. 2) Rebase and merge: - You rebase the feature branch onto the target branch, then fast-forward merge. - Result: linear history, preserves individual commits (but rewritten hashes if rebased). Manual approach: - git checkout feature - git rebase main - git checkout main - git merge --ff-only feature 3) Squash and merge: - You take all commits from the feature branch and squash them into a single commit on main. - History is linear and condensed; intermediate commits don’t appear on main. Manual approach: - git checkout main - git merge --squash feature - git commit Or interactively rebase to squash before merging. Each style trades off detail vs cleanliness. Squash-and-merge is often used when intermediate commits are noisy (“WIP”, “fix typo”), but it discards that granularity on the main branch history.
But interactive rebase can also squash, right? So why would someone use merge --squash?

Yes, interactive rebase can squash (and do much more), but merge --squash has a different feel: - git merge --squash feature applies the net changes from feature as one combined change without creating a merge commit and without bringing over the individual commit metadata. You then create one new commit on the current branch. - Interactive rebase -i on the feature branch rewrites the feature branch commits themselves, producing a cleaned-up sequence that still contains multiple commits (or one, if you squash everything). Then you merge/fast-forward that branch. Practical differences: - With merge --squash, the feature branch commits do not become ancestors of main. Git won’t “know” that feature was merged in the same way. If you later merge the same feature branch again, Git may try to reapply changes because the commits aren’t shared (though content might be recognized). - With rebase + ff merge, the commits (rewritten) do become ancestors of main. Future merges from that branch are cleaner if it continues. So merge --squash is good for “one-and-done” topic branches. Rebase is better if you care about commit-level history or continuing to build on that branch.
That “Git won’t know it was merged” part confuses me. Doesn’t it know based on file content?

Git primarily reasons about history using commit ancestry, not file content similarity. When you do a normal merge (with a merge commit) or a fast-forward merge, the commits from the feature branch become part of main’s history (ancestors). Then Git can see “these commits are already included.” When you do merge --squash, you create a new commit whose content might match the combined changes, but it has a different ancestry: it doesn’t have the feature commits as ancestors. So if you later try to merge feature again, Git can’t conclude “already merged” based on ancestry. It may attempt to merge again, and you might get conflicts or duplicated changes depending on what changed since. Git can sometimes detect already-applied patches via “patch-id” in certain contexts (like git cherry-pick --skip or some rebase cases), but the core merge machinery uses commit graph relationships.
So if I squash, I should probably delete the feature branch after?

Often that’s the simplest. If you squash-merge a topic branch and you’re done with it, deleting it avoids confusion later. If you keep it and continue committing on it, you’ll have a branch whose old commits aren’t in main, but whose changes are. That can lead to messy future merges. If you need to continue the work after squash-merge, a common approach is: - create a new branch off main for the next chunk, or - reset the old branch to main (git checkout feature; git reset --hard main) and then continue from there (but that discards the old branch’s unique commit history).
Back to rebase: what exactly is the “don’t rebase published commits” rule? Like what goes wrong?

If you rebase commits that exist in another clone (or anything else that depends on them), you create a divergence where the same logical changes exist as different commits. Example: You have branch feature with commits D and E. Somewhere else there’s a reference to those commits (maybe you pushed them to a remote, or another clone fetched them). If you rebase feature onto a new base, D and E become D’ and E’. Now: - Your feature points to D’ and E’. - The remote (or other clone) still has D and E. If you then push, you can’t do a simple fast-forward push because history changed. You might force push, which rewrites the remote branch pointer to D’/E’. But anyone who had the old D/E will now have to reconcile. What goes wrong for them: - If they had built work on top of E, their commits now have parent E. After you force update the branch to E’, their branch and yours diverge. They may need to rebase their work onto E’ or do a complicated merge. - If they try to pull normally, they might get merge commits or errors depending on configuration. - It can create duplicated commits if they rebase without understanding, because Git sees D/E and D’/E’ as different commits. So the rule is about avoiding unnecessary pain from rewriting shared history.
If I accidentally rebased something and pushed it, is there a way to recover?

Usually yes, as long as the old commits haven’t been garbage-collected and you can find their hashes. Steps: 1) Use git reflog to find the previous position of the branch before the rebase. - git reflog show feature You’ll see entries like: feature@{1}: rebase finished: returning to refs/heads/feature feature@{2}: checkout: moving from ... feature@{3}: commit: ... And importantly, a line for “rebase: …” with the old HEAD. 2) Reset the branch back to the old commit: - git checkout feature - git reset --hard <oldhash> 3) Force-push back if needed: - git push --force-with-lease origin feature If others already fetched the rebased version, you need to decide which history to keep. But purely technically, reflog is your time machine locally. On a remote, some servers keep a reflog-like record, but not always accessible; you might still recover if someone else has the old commits.
What’s the difference between --force and --force-with-lease?

Both allow pushing non-fast-forward updates (rewriting the remote branch pointer), but --force-with-lease is safer. - --force: “Update the remote ref no matter what it currently points to.” - --force-with-lease: “Update the remote ref only if it still points to what I think it points to.” So with --force-with-lease, Git checks that the remote branch hasn’t moved since you last fetched. If it has moved, the push is rejected, preventing you from accidentally overwriting someone else’s newer commits. It’s a good default if you ever force-push.
I’ve heard about “rebase onto” and also “--onto” option. When would I use that?

git rebase --onto is for rebasing a range of commits from one base to a different base, especially when the branch point isn’t the right starting point. General form: - git rebase --onto <newbase> <upstream> <branch> Meaning: - Take commits reachable from <branch> but not from <upstream> (i.e., the range <upstream>.. <branch>), and replay them onto <newbase>. Example scenario: You branched feature from old main, but actually you want it based on another branch (say base2), or you accidentally started feature from the wrong starting commit. Graph: main:   A---B---C---D base2:  A---B---X---Y feature:        C---D---E---F   (feature started at C but should be on Y) You want E and F (or maybe D/E/F depending) onto Y. If feature includes commits D/E/F, and you want to move the part after C: - git rebase --onto base2 C feature This takes commits after C on feature and replays them onto base2 (Y). It’s also useful to split a branch: move a subset of commits to another base.
How does Git decide what the “upstream” is if I just run git rebase main?

If you run git rebase main while on branch feature, then: - <newbase> is main - <upstream> is the fork-point between feature and main (conceptually the merge base), though there are nuances. Git finds the commits that are in feature but not in main (main..feature) and replays them onto main. However, if you previously merged main into feature, the naive merge-base might not represent the original fork point you expect. That’s why there’s an option: - git rebase --fork-point main Or config rebase.forkPoint, which tries to find where the branch actually diverged based on reflog of the upstream branch (often useful when branches were rebased or force-updated upstream).
Wait, so merges into the feature branch can mess with the “list of commits to replay”?

They can. If you merge main into feature repeatedly, the feature branch history contains merge commits that incorporate main’s changes. When you later rebase, Git has to decide which commits are “yours” and which are already in main. Usually it works fine, but the commit selection can get surprising if: - You merged other branches into feature. - You cherry-picked commits from main into feature. - Upstream history was rewritten. The typical advice is: either - keep feature branches rebased (avoid merging main into them), or - keep them merged (avoid rebasing them), rather than mixing both constantly. But it’s not forbidden; it just increases complexity.
Conflicts: do I get more conflicts with rebase or merge?

It depends on timing and how often you integrate. - A merge integrates all differences at once: you resolve conflicts once for that merge commit. - A rebase might require resolving conflicts multiple times—once per commit being replayed—because each commit is applied separately. That can feel more tedious. However: - Rebasing frequently (small divergence) can reduce conflict size, even if it happens more often. - Merging frequently also reduces conflict size. - If your commits are well-scoped, rebase conflicts can be easier because you’re resolving in the context of a small change. - If you have a big “mega-commit” or messy commit series, rebasing can be painful. There’s also git rerere (reuse recorded resolution), which can help both merge and rebase by remembering how you resolved a conflict and reapplying that resolution when it sees the same conflict again.
I keep seeing rerere mentioned but never used it. What is it exactly?

git rerere stands for “reuse recorded resolution.” When enabled, Git records how you resolved a particular conflict (based on the conflict hunks). If the same conflict occurs again later (e.g., during another rebase step, another merge, or even a cherry-pick), Git can automatically apply the previously recorded resolution. Enable it: - git config --global rerere.enabled true Workflow: 1) You merge/rebase and hit conflicts. 2) You resolve conflicts and git add the resolved files. 3) Git records the before/after resolution. 4) Next time a similar conflict appears, Git can auto-resolve it (or partially). It’s especially useful if you: - repeatedly rebase a long-lived branch, - repeatedly merge main into a branch, - do complex sequences of cherry-picks.
If rebase replays commits one by one, can it change the meaning of the commits? Like could tests pass before but fail after?

Yes, rebasing can expose hidden dependencies between commits. If your commit series assumes a particular base state, replaying onto a different base can cause: - conflicts, - subtle behavioral changes, - failing tests, - changes in performance or APIs. Also, if your commits were not independently valid (e.g., commit 1 breaks build, commit 2 fixes it), rebasing still preserves that order, but the new base might make the brokenness worse or introduce new conflicts at commit 1. That’s why it’s helpful to make commits: - small, - logically consistent, - ideally “buildable”/testable per commit if you care about bisecting. Interactive rebase can help reorder/fixup commits so the series makes sense.
Speaking of interactive rebase, what’s the difference between “squash” and “fixup”?

In interactive rebase (git rebase -i), you can mark commits with commands. Two commonly used: - squash: combine the commit into the previous one, and open an editor to combine/modify commit messages. - fixup: combine the commit into the previous one, but discard the fixup commit’s message (keeps the previous commit message). It’s faster/cleaner when the message doesn’t matter. Example todo list: pick a1 Add parser pick b2 Fix typo pick c3 Add tests You might do: pick a1 Add parser fixup b2 Fix typo pick c3 Add tests Then b2 is folded into a1 without prompting for a message. There’s also autosquash: - If you name a commit “fixup! <subject>” or “squash! <subject>” - Then git rebase -i --autosquash will automatically place it next to the target commit and mark it fixup/squash.
That’s nice. But I’ve also heard rebasing can be dangerous because it can lose commits. Is that true?

It can feel dangerous, but Git is usually recoverable if you know where to look. During rebase, Git: - moves your branch pointer around, - creates new commits, - and if something goes wrong you might end up detached HEAD or with conflicts. Commits are rarely “lost” immediately. Old commits remain in the object database and are referenced in reflog for some time. So you can often recover with: - git reflog - git reset - git rebase --abort (to return to pre-rebase state) - git rebase --continue (after resolving conflicts) - git rebase --skip (skip a problematic commit) The real danger is force-pushing rewritten history and causing confusion, not irrecoverable data loss.
What’s detached HEAD and how does it relate to rebase?

Detached HEAD means HEAD points directly to a commit, not to a branch name. You can still make commits, but they won’t belong to a named branch unless you create one. Rebase itself usually keeps you on a branch, but you might end up detached HEAD if you: - check out a specific commit (git checkout <hash>), - or interrupt operations and end up at an intermediate state, - or use commands that check out commits directly. During rebase, Git temporarily checks out commits as it applies them, but it manages the branch ref. If you abort, it returns you. If you get stuck and check out something manually mid-rebase, you can end up detached. If you ever make commits in detached HEAD and want to keep them: - git switch -c new-branch-name (or git checkout -b new-branch-name)
You said earlier that merge commits record integration points. Is that ever useful beyond “history purity”?

Yes. Merge commits can encode meaningful information: - They indicate that two lines of development were combined intentionally at a specific time. - If you use merge commits for features, you can revert a whole feature by reverting the merge commit (git revert -m 1 <merge-hash>), which can be handy. (Though reverting merge commits has nuances.) - They can help answer questions like: “When did changes from branch X land?” because that merge commit is a boundary. - For some debugging, seeing merges can clarify how changes flowed. That said, you can also get some of these benefits with tags, structured commit messages, or careful squashing, but merge commits provide it structurally.
Reverting a merge commit sounds scary. What does -m 1 mean?

A merge commit has two (or more) parents. When you revert a merge, Git needs to know which parent should be considered the “mainline” (the baseline you want to keep). - git revert -m 1 <merge_commit> means: treat parent 1 as mainline; undo the changes that were introduced by merging the other parent(s) into parent 1. Parent numbering: - Parent 1 is typically the branch you were on when you ran git merge. - Parent 2 is the branch you merged in. So reverting a merge commit doesn’t “delete” history; it creates a new commit that undoes the net effect of that merge relative to the chosen mainline. It can be tricky because later merges can reintroduce changes, and conflicts can happen. But it’s a powerful tool.
If I rebase instead of merge, does that make reverting harder?

It changes the shape of the problem. - With merge commits, you can often revert the merge commit as a single action to undo an entire feature integration (depending on how it was merged and subsequent changes). - With a rebased + fast-forward history, the feature’s commits are just a linear set on main. To revert “the feature,” you might revert a range of commits (possibly multiple revert commits), or revert a single squashed commit if you squashed. So: - If you want “one commit to revert a feature,” squash-merge gives you that. - If you want “structural revert of a feature branch,” merge commits can help. - With rebase preserving individual commits, revert is still doable but may be multiple commits.
Okay. I’d like a practical rule of thumb. Suppose I have a feature branch and main is moving. What should I do day-to-day?

A practical, simple approach (for a solo, local workflow) is: - While you’re still developing on your feature branch and it hasn’t been shared widely: rebase it onto main periodically to keep up to date: - git fetch origin - git rebase origin/main Resolve conflicts as needed. - Before integrating back into main, optionally interactive rebase to clean up: - git rebase -i origin/main Squash/fixup/reword as needed. - Then integrate in a way that matches your desired main history: - If you want linear history: fast-forward merge after rebase. - If you want explicit integration commits: merge with a merge commit. If the feature branch has already been shared and others depend on its commits, prefer merging main into it rather than rebasing, unless everyone coordinating agrees to rewriting.
You mentioned “fetch origin” and “origin/main”. Why not just “git pull”?

git pull is basically: - git fetch - then git merge (or rebase, depending on config) It’s convenient, but it can hide what’s happening. When learning, it’s often clearer to do fetch + merge/rebase explicitly. For example: - git pull (default) might create a merge commit unexpectedly. - git pull --rebase will rebase your local commits on top of fetched upstream, which is often desirable but can surprise you if you weren’t expecting a rebase. Using: - git fetch origin - git rebase origin/main makes it explicit: you are rebasing your current branch onto the updated remote-tracking branch.
If I set pull.rebase=true, does that mean it will always rebase? Is that safe?

Setting pull.rebase=true makes git pull perform a rebase instead of a merge when integrating upstream changes into your current branch. It’s “safe” if you understand the implications: - It will rewrite your local commits (the ones not in upstream) by replaying them. - If you have local merge commits, rebasing can get more complex (though there’s --rebase-merges). It’s typically safe for local branches where you don’t want merge commits from pulls. But if you have a branch where you intentionally want merges, you can override per-invocation: - git pull --no-rebase There are also related settings: - pull.ff only (only fast-forward, otherwise fail) - rebase.autoStash true (stash local changes before rebase, pop after)
What is autoStash? Is it like git stash?

Yes. During rebase (or pull --rebase), Git requires a clean working tree for safety. If you have uncommitted changes, it will usually refuse. With rebase.autoStash enabled, Git will: 1) automatically create a stash of your uncommitted changes, 2) perform the rebase, 3) re-apply the stash afterward. Enable: - git config --global rebase.autoStash true It’s convenient, but be aware: - If your uncommitted changes conflict with changes introduced during the rebase, applying the stash may produce conflicts. - It’s still better to commit or stash intentionally when doing complex operations, but autoStash reduces friction.
You said “rebase-merges.” What does that do? I thought rebase removes merges.

Classic rebase flattens history by default, and it doesn’t preserve merge commits; it linearizes. git rebase --rebase-merges (older name: --preserve-merges, deprecated) attempts to rebase while preserving the branch structure and merge commits, essentially “replaying merges” too. Why use it: - You have a branch with meaningful merge commits (e.g., you merged topic branches into your branch). - You still want to move the whole structure onto a new base. It can be more complex and can produce different results if the original merges were conflict-resolved in particular ways. But it exists for advanced cases.
If rebase can preserve merges, does that mean it’s not necessarily “linear history”?

Exactly. Rebase is a mechanism for transplanting commits; the default mode produces a linear series, but with --rebase-merges, you can keep a non-linear structure while still changing the base. So “rebase = linear” is a common shorthand, but not a universal truth.
Let’s talk about conflict resolution. During rebase, I often see messages like “Applying: …” and then conflict. How do I properly continue?

Typical rebase conflict workflow: 1) Start rebase: - git rebase origin/main 2) If conflict occurs: Git stops and tells you which files are conflicted. 3) Inspect status: - git status It will say “You are currently rebasing” and list unmerged paths. 4) Open conflicted files, resolve markers: <<<<<<<, =======, >>>>>>> 5) Mark resolved: - git add <files> 6) Continue: - git rebase --continue If you realize you don’t want to proceed: - Abort: - git rebase --abort If a commit is problematic and you want to skip it: - git rebase --skip Be cautious: skipping means that commit’s changes won’t be applied.
During merge conflicts, I sometimes use a merge tool. Does that also work for rebase conflicts?

Yes. Rebase conflicts are still merge conflicts under the hood (Git is trying to apply a patch with 3-way merge). You can use: - git mergetool if configured, or your editor’s Git integration. The conflict markers and resolution process are the same. After resolving, you git add and continue.
I’ve also seen “git cherry-pick” described similarly to rebase. How are they related?

Cherry-pick and rebase are closely related conceptually: - git cherry-pick <commit> takes a commit and applies its changes on top of your current HEAD, creating a new commit. - git rebase essentially cherry-picks a sequence of commits onto a new base (plus some extra logic to find which commits, handle conflicts, etc.). So you can think of rebase as “automated cherry-picking of a range.” Both rewrite commit hashes because they create new commits.
So if I want to move one commit from one branch to another, cherry-pick is better?

Usually yes. Use cherry-pick when: - You want a specific commit (or a small set) from elsewhere. - You don’t want the entire branch history. - You want to backport a fix. Use rebase when: - You want to move the entire line of development (the commits on your branch) onto a new base. Cherry-pick can also be used in batches: - git cherry-pick A..B (range) But then you’re basically doing a manual mini-rebase.
What about “git merge --no-ff”? How does that fit into all this?

git merge --no-ff forces a merge commit even when a fast-forward is possible. Normally, if main can fast-forward to feature, Git would just move the pointer and you wouldn’t see a merge commit. With --no-ff, you get an explicit merge commit, preserving a visible “this feature was merged here” marker. So it’s a way to keep merge commits as integration points even for short-lived branches. Compare: - Fast-forward merge: linear, but no explicit merge marker. - --no-ff merge: non-linear (or at least includes merge commits), with explicit integration commits.
If I really care about a clean history, should I avoid merge commits entirely?

Not necessarily. “Clean” can mean different things: - Clean as in “linear and simple”: you might avoid merge commits (except maybe for release merges). - Clean as in “accurately structured and informative”: you might prefer merge commits with meaningful messages. Some people consider “clean” to be: - no noisy WIP commits (solved by squashing or interactive rebase), - no accidental merge commits from pulling (solved by pull --rebase), - and a consistent integration style. You can have a clean history with merge commits if they’re intentional and not clutter.
How do I inspect the history to actually see what’s going on? git log confuses me.

A few useful commands: 1) Visualize graph: - git log --oneline --graph --decorate --all This shows branches and merges in ASCII. 2) See only first-parent history (useful to see mainline progression when merges exist): - git log --first-parent --oneline 3) Find merge base: - git merge-base main feature 4) Show commits on feature not in main: - git log main..feature --oneline 5) Show commits in main not in feature: - git log feature..main --oneline These help you reason about divergence and what will be replayed/merged.
main..feature notation is still weird to me. What does A..B mean?

A..B means “commits reachable from B that are not reachable from A.” So: - main..feature = commits that are on feature but not on main (the unique commits you’d bring in when merging feature into main). - feature..main = commits on main but not on feature. There’s also A...B (three dots), which means “commits reachable from either A or B but not both” (symmetric difference). Useful to see both sides’ unique commits.
If I have a feature branch and I want to update it with main’s changes, what are the exact commands for merge vs rebase?

Assuming you’re on feature and you want the latest main from origin: First: - git fetch origin Merge approach: - git merge origin/main This creates a merge commit if there’s divergence. Rebase approach: - git rebase origin/main This replays your feature commits on top of origin/main. If you have uncommitted changes, either stash or enable autoStash.
Sometimes after rebase, I see that my branch has diverged from origin/feature and push is rejected. That’s because of the new hashes, right?

Correct. After rebase, your local feature has rewritten commits, so your local branch is no longer a fast-forward of origin/feature. A normal push would be rejected to prevent rewriting remote history. To update the remote branch pointer to your rebased history, you’d need: - git push --force-with-lease origin feature But only do that if you’re sure you want to rewrite the remote branch. If you don’t want to force-push, then you shouldn’t rebase a branch that’s already pushed (or you should use a different strategy, like merging instead).
Is there a way to rebase without having to force push?

If the branch is only local (not pushed), no force push needed. If it’s already pushed and you rebase, then by definition you’ve rewritten history, so updating the remote requires a non-fast-forward push (force). The only way to avoid forcing is: don’t rewrite what’s already on the remote. Use merge, or create a new branch name for the rebased version (push it as a new branch), leaving the old one intact. Example: - git push origin feature:feature-rebased Now you have a new remote branch name with the rebased commits, without rewriting the old one.
That’s interesting. So I could keep both histories and compare?

Yes. Keeping both can be useful for learning or for migration. You can compare: - git log --oneline --graph --decorate --all and see both references. You can also compare content: - git diff origin/feature..origin/feature-rebased Often there will be no diff (same final content), even though commit hashes differ.
Wait, if the content is identical, why does Git treat it as different? Only because of hashes?

Git treats commits as identities. Even if two commits produce identical file trees, they’re still different commits if their metadata or parents differ, hence different hashes. Also, two different sequences of commits can lead to the same final tree. Git cares about the commit graph, not just snapshots. That said, Git can sometimes detect “equivalent” changes via patch-id (like in some rebase/cherry-pick scenarios), but commit identity is still primary.
You mentioned patch-id earlier. What is that?

patch-id is a way to identify a change by its patch content rather than commit hash. It hashes the diff itself (with some normalization), so the same change applied as different commits can be recognized as “the same patch.” You can compute patch-ids via plumbing commands, but most users encounter it indirectly: - During rebase, Git may skip commits whose patch already exists upstream (depending on options). - Some tooling uses patch-id to avoid applying duplicates. It’s not a perfect equivalence (context matters), but it’s a useful heuristic.
If I have already merged main into my feature branch, and then I try to rebase, I sometimes get weird duplicate commits or conflicts. Is that because of patch-id mismatches?

It can be due to a few factors: - If you merged main into feature, your branch contains merge commits and possibly commits from main are now reachable. When rebasing, Git tries to identify which commits are unique to feature. Complex graphs can make this less straightforward. - If you cherry-picked commits from main into feature earlier, then later rebase onto main, Git might see those commits as duplicates (or not) depending on patch similarity and metadata. - If upstream changed in a way that makes patches apply differently, the rebase might not detect equivalence. Sometimes using: - git rebase --onto origin/main <fork-point> or - git rebase --fork-point origin/main helps. But the simplest is: pick one approach (merge updates or rebase updates) and stick with it for that branch.
How do I find the fork point manually?

The basic fork point is the merge base: - git merge-base main feature That gives you the best common ancestor commit hash. If you want to see it: - git show $(git merge-base main feature) If you want a list of commits since fork: - git log --oneline $(git merge-base main feature)..feature However, if upstream branch tips were rewritten, merge-base might not reflect the original fork point you “meant.” In that case, --fork-point tries to do better by using reflog info.
Suppose I want to clean up commits before sharing them. Interactive rebase seems right. What’s a good flow?

A common flow: 1) Make commits as you work (even messy). 2) Before sharing, run: - git fetch origin - git rebase -i origin/main 3) In the todo list: - reorder commits to logical order - squash/fixup small follow-ups - reword messages - drop accidental commits - edit commits to split them (using “edit” and then reset) 4) After rebase finishes, run tests, ensure everything is correct. 5) Push (if the branch was already pushed, you’ll need --force-with-lease). If you need to split a commit during interactive rebase: - mark it as edit - when stopped at that commit: - git reset HEAD^ (or git reset --mixed HEAD^) This uncommits the changes but keeps them in working tree - stage parts and commit multiple times - git rebase --continue
I’ve never been comfortable with reset. What’s the difference between --soft, --mixed, --hard?

reset moves the current branch pointer (and optionally updates index and working tree). - git reset --soft <commit> Moves HEAD to <commit>, but keeps index and working tree as-is. So changes from the “removed” commits remain staged. - git reset --mixed <commit> (default) Moves HEAD and resets the index to match <commit>, but leaves working tree changes. So changes become unstaged in your working directory. - git reset --hard <commit> Moves HEAD, resets index, and resets working tree to match <commit>. This discards uncommitted changes (dangerous if you haven’t saved them). In the “split a commit” workflow, --mixed is common because it keeps the changes but unstages them so you can re-add selectively.
If I do a rebase and then want to undo it, is reset the right tool?

Often, yes, but the easiest is: - git rebase --abort (if the rebase is still in progress) If the rebase already finished, use reflog to find the pre-rebase HEAD and reset back: - git reflog Find the entry like “rebase (finish)” and the previous head. - git reset --hard HEAD@{n} That restores your branch pointer to the previous state.
During rebase, sometimes Git says “No changes — Patch already applied.” What does that mean?

It means the patch represented by a commit you’re trying to replay results in no net change when applied to the new base—likely because the changes already exist there. This can happen if: - the commit was cherry-picked elsewhere, - the same changes were introduced independently on the new base, - or conflict resolution ended up making the commit redundant. Git might then skip creating a new commit for it. You may also see options: - --reapply-cherry-picks to force applying even if Git thinks it’s already applied (useful if detection is wrong, but can cause duplicates).
How do I decide whether to reapply cherry-picks or not?

Default behavior is usually fine. You’d consider --reapply-cherry-picks if: - You know a commit was cherry-picked and you want to keep it as a distinct commit in the rebased branch anyway (maybe for traceability). - Git incorrectly decides a commit is already applied (patch-id collision or subtle differences) and skips it, but you actually needed it. If you use it casually, you risk duplicating changes that are already in the base. So it’s best used when you have a specific reason and you’re verifying diffs afterward.
Can you explain “merge commits are meaningful” vs “merge commits are noise”? I see both arguments.

It depends on what the merge commit represents. Meaningful merge commits: - They correspond to a deliberate integration of a coherent set of changes. - The merge message or context tells you what was integrated. - They help you see when multiple lines came together. Noisy merge commits: - They’re created accidentally by frequent git pull without rebase. - They merge small upstream updates repeatedly, cluttering history. - They don’t represent a conceptual event; they’re just “I pulled.” So one can prefer merge commits but still dislike “accidental merge commits.” That’s why some people set pull.rebase=true but still do explicit merges when they mean to.
That makes a lot of sense. So if I’m just keeping my local branch up to date, rebasing avoids those “pull merges.”

Exactly. Rebasing for updates keeps your branch linear and avoids repeated merge commits like “Merge branch 'main' into feature” that don’t add conceptual value. Then when you integrate back, you can choose whether you want a merge commit or a fast-forward.
Are there cases where rebase is actually worse even for local branches?

Yes. A few cases: - If your branch has many merge commits that you care about (unless you use --rebase-merges). - If you’re in the middle of something and have lots of uncommitted changes; rebase requires a clean state (autoStash can help). - If your commits are huge and conflict-prone; rebasing will make you resolve conflicts per commit, which can be time-consuming. - If you rely on commit hashes as stable references (e.g., in documentation or scripts). Rebasing will change them. - If you’re using signed commits: rebasing recreates commits and can require re-signing (depending on config and workflow).
Signed commits—good point. Does rebase preserve GPG signatures?

Not automatically, because rebased commits are new commits, and their signatures would no longer be valid. You can re-sign during rebase: - git rebase --exec 'git commit --amend --no-edit -S' But that can be cumbersome. There are also options like: - git rebase --committer-date-is-author-date (different metadata behavior) - and config commit.gpgSign true to sign commits by default. But the core is: if you care about signatures on individual commits, rewriting history implies re-signing those commits.
What about commit timestamps? Rebasing changes them?

Typically: - Author date stays the same (the original author timestamp). - Committer date changes to the time you rebased (because you’re creating commits anew). You can see both with: - git log --pretty=fuller There are options to control date behavior if you really care, but most people accept that committer date reflects when the commit was applied/recreated.
If I run git log, can I tell that a commit was rebased?

Not always reliably, but hints include: - Committer date much later than author date. - The commit hash differs from what you remember. - The commit appears in a different location in history. But Git doesn’t store “this was rebased” as a flag. It’s just a commit object. If you want traceability, you can: - include references in commit messages (like “(cherry picked from …)” is auto-added by git cherry-pick -x) - or use notes (git notes) to attach metadata without changing commits.
git notes sounds cool. Could notes help with rebase tracking?

Potentially, yes. git notes lets you attach extra information to commits without modifying the commit object itself. But notes are not always shared by default. You can push notes refs, but it’s extra setup: - Notes live under refs/notes/* - You can configure fetching/pushing notes. For personal tracking, notes can be useful. For cross-clone sharing, it depends on whether you consistently propagate notes.
Let’s do an example. Suppose I have: main: A-B-C feature: C-D-E Then main gets F, so main: A-B-C-F If I rebase feature onto main, I get C? Actually I get A-B-C-F-D’-E’. Right?
Right. After rebase, feature will be based on F, so the chain is: A-B-C-F-D’-E’ C is still in the history because it’s an ancestor of F. Your new commits D’ and E’ sit on top of F.
And if instead I merged main into feature, feature would become C-D-E-M with M merging F?
Yes. More precisely: main: A-B-C-F feature before: A-B-C-D-E Merge main into feature creates M with parents E and F: A-B-C-D-E-M \   / F- (ASCII gets cramped, but that’s the idea.)
Now if I later merge feature back into main, what happens in each case?

Case 1: You rebased feature onto main first. main: A-B-C-F feature: A-B-C-F-D’-E’ Now merging feature into main can fast-forward (since main is ancestor of feature), assuming main hasn’t moved: - git checkout main - git merge --ff-only feature Result: main: A-B-C-F-D’-E’ Case 2: You merged main into feature (creating M), without rebasing. main: A-B-C-F feature: A-B-C-D-E-M (with M merging F) Now merging feature back into main will likely create another merge commit (unless you do something special), because main and feature have diverged: - git checkout main - git merge feature Result: a merge commit N on main with parents F and M. History will have two merge commits: M (main into feature) and N (feature into main). This is one reason some people avoid merging main into feature repeatedly: it can create “criss-cross merges.”
Criss-cross merges—that sounds like a mess. What are the practical downsides?

Criss-cross merges can make history harder to reason about and can make future merges more complex, because there are multiple merge bases and repeated integration points. Git can still handle it, but: - merge-base computations can yield multiple candidates - conflict resolution can get more confusing - tools and humans have a harder time understanding what happened If you frequently update feature with main and then merge back, rebasing feature onto main (instead of merging main into it) avoids those intermediate merge commits and keeps integration simpler.
Are there cases where merging main into feature is actually preferred?

Yes, especially when you don’t want to rewrite the feature branch history (because it’s shared or because you want to preserve exact integration steps), or when the branch is long-lived and you prefer explicit periodic integration. Also, if you have a branch that represents a release line or maintenance line, merges can be a better representation of how changes were integrated over time. But for many short-lived topic branches, rebasing onto main is a common way to keep them current without polluting history.
I want to understand the “published commits” rule better without involving other people. If I push to a remote for backup, does that count as “published”?

Yes, in the sense that once a commit is pushed to a remote, you’ve created another reference to that history. Even if you’re the only one using it, you might later have: - another clone on another machine, - an automated process that fetched it, - or simply the remote as a stable reference. If you rebase and force-push, you’re rewriting that remote reference. That’s not inherently bad if you’re the only consumer and you’re comfortable force-pushing, but it’s still “published” in that there’s now an external reference you might need to reconcile with. So the rule becomes: if anything else might depend on those commit IDs, be cautious.
If I’m alone, is it okay to force-push rebased branches all the time?

Usually yes, as long as you’re disciplined and aware of the consequences. Good practices: - Use --force-with-lease rather than --force. - Avoid force-pushing main unless you’re absolutely sure (rewriting main can break clones and tags). - Consider keeping tags for important states (e.g., “backup-before-rewrite”) if you’re about to do a big rewrite. - If you have multiple clones, fetch frequently so your “lease” is accurate.
You mentioned tags. If I tag a commit and then rebase, what happens to the tag?

Tags point to specific commits by hash. - If you rebase and create new commits, the tag still points to the old commit (the pre-rebase commit). - If that old commit becomes unreachable from branches, it can still be reachable via the tag, so it won’t be garbage-collected. So tags can act as anchors to preserve old history. If you want the tag to refer to the rebased version, you’d need to move the tag (which is also history rewriting for the tag ref): - git tag -f <tagname> <newhash> - git push --force origin <tagname> (Force needed because tags are refs too.)
Is that why people say “never move tags”?

Yes. Many treat tags (especially annotated tags for releases) as immutable pointers. Moving them can be confusing because tags are often used as stable references. But for personal or temporary tags, moving is fine if you understand it’s rewriting a reference.
What about “git pull --rebase” vs “git fetch; git rebase”? Any difference?

Functionally similar, but there are subtle differences: - git pull --rebase does fetch and then rebase in one step. It rebases your local commits on top of the updated upstream tracking branch for your current branch (or specified remote/branch). - git fetch; git rebase origin/main is explicit about what you’re rebasing onto. pull --rebase can be more convenient, but you need to be aware of: - what upstream is configured for the branch, - whether it will rebase against the branch you expect. When learning or when working with multiple remotes/branches, explicit fetch + rebase reduces surprises.
Sometimes I run git pull and it says “Already up to date.” But I know main moved. How is that possible?

Usually one of these: - You pulled on a branch that tracks a different upstream than you think. Check: - git branch -vv It shows tracking info. - You didn’t fetch the remote that changed (maybe changes are on another remote name). - git remote -v - git fetch <remote> - You’re on main but main isn’t tracking origin/main (or tracking is misconfigured). - Or you’re looking at a different “main” (local vs remote). Remember: - main is your local branch. - origin/main is your remote-tracking branch updated by fetch. So if origin/main moved but you didn’t fetch, your local view might be stale.
That branch -vv command is useful. What should I look for?

Example output: main    123abcd [origin/main] Commit message feature 456beef [origin/feature: ahead 3, behind 1] Another message Look for: - [origin/main] indicates main tracks origin/main. - “ahead/behind” indicates divergence: you have commits not pushed (ahead), or remote has commits you don’t have (behind). If you’re behind, you need to merge/rebase/pull to incorporate.
If my feature is “ahead 3, behind 1”, what’s the best fix?

It means: - You have 3 local commits not on origin/feature. - origin/feature has 1 commit you don’t have. You can: 1) Merge remote changes into your local feature: - git fetch origin - git merge origin/feature This keeps history and avoids rewriting. 2) Rebase your local commits on top of origin/feature: - git fetch origin - git rebase origin/feature This rewrites your 3 local commits, but results in a linear history and then you can push normally (fast-forward) because you didn’t rewrite origin/feature; you replayed on top of it. However, if you had previously pushed your 3 commits and then rebased them, you’d need force push. In this scenario (ahead 3, behind 1), if the 3 commits are only local, rebasing is often a nice solution.
Wait, after rebasing onto origin/feature, can I push without force?

Yes, if you rebased only local commits that weren’t on the remote yet. Your branch becomes a fast-forward of origin/feature. Then: - git push origin feature will succeed as a fast-forward update. Force is only needed when you rewrite commits that are already on the remote branch.
That’s a key point. So rebase is “safe” if I haven’t pushed those commits yet.
Exactly. The safe zone is: rewriting local-only history. Once commits are pushed and might be referenced elsewhere, rewriting becomes more consequential.
Can we talk about merge strategies? Like “ours”, “theirs”, etc. Does rebase have equivalents?

Git has merge strategies and options that influence conflict resolution: For merge: - git merge -s ours <branch> means “record a merge but keep our content” (special case). - During conflicts, you can choose: - git checkout --ours <file> - git checkout --theirs <file> (or git restore equivalents) For rebase: - Rebase uses merge machinery to apply patches, and you can pass strategy options: - git rebase -X theirs <upstream> - git rebase -X ours <upstream> These “favor” one side in conflicts, but be careful: “ours/theirs” refer to different sides depending on operation context. Also: - git rebase --strategy-option=theirs But using these blindly can hide real conflicts and cause incorrect resolutions. Better to use when you understand the specific conflict pattern.
The “ours/theirs” meaning always trips me. Can you clarify?

It is confusing because “ours” and “theirs” are relative to the operation. In a merge while on branch current: - “ours” = current branch (the one you’re merging into) - “theirs” = the branch being merged in In a rebase: - You’re applying commits from your branch onto upstream. Depending on the step, “ours/theirs” can feel inverted relative to what you expect, because Git is effectively doing merges of each commit’s changes. A more reliable approach is to inspect: - git status - and the conflict markers in the files to ensure you’re choosing the correct side. Also, modern Git suggests using: - git checkout --conflict=merge <file> - or using a mergetool, rather than relying on ours/theirs shortcuts unless you’re sure.
If rebase is like cherry-picking, can I use “git am” concepts? Or is that different?

git am applies patches from mailbox files (like emailed patches). It’s similar in that it applies diffs and creates commits. Under the hood, it’s another way of “replaying” changes. Rebase/cherry-pick/am are all in the family of “apply a patch and create a new commit,” with different sources: - cherry-pick: existing commit objects - rebase: sequence of commits - am: patch files So conceptually related, but you usually don’t need git am unless you’re working with patch files.
I want to be able to explain rebase vs merge to someone else. Can you give me a concise explanation with a memorable analogy?

One analogy: - Merge is like tying two ropes together with a knot: you keep both ropes intact and you can see where they joined (merge commit). - Rebase is like untying one rope’s end and weaving it onto the end of the other rope: the rope becomes one continuous line, but the woven section is newly formed (new commit hashes). Or more Git-specific: - Merge records “these histories met here.” - Rebase rewrites “as if my work started from the new base.”
Nice. Another question: if I rebase a branch, does it affect main at all?

Rebasing a branch only moves that branch pointer (and creates new commits). It doesn’t change main unless you explicitly rebase main (or merge the rebased branch into main). So: - git checkout feature - git rebase main changes feature, not main. But if you later fast-forward main to feature, then main’s history will include those rebased commits.
What if I accidentally run git rebase while on main?

Then you’re rebasing main onto whatever upstream you specify, which can rewrite main’s commits if main has unique commits. If you ran: - git rebase origin/main while on main, and your local main has commits not on origin/main, Git will replay them. That might be okay locally, but if you then force-push main you’d be rewriting a primary branch. If it was accidental, you can: - git rebase --abort (if in progress) or use reflog to reset main back.
I think I’m getting it. But there’s still one thing: what does “merge base” mean exactly?

The merge base of two commits/branches is their best common ancestor—the commit from which both histories diverged. Git uses the merge base as the reference point for 3-way merges: - base: common ancestor - ours: current branch tip - theirs: other branch tip Then it computes changes from base→ours and base→theirs and tries to combine them. You can see it: - git merge-base A B In simple cases, it’s the single fork point. In complex histories with criss-cross merges, there can be multiple merge bases, and Git chooses one (or uses recursive strategy to synthesize).
If merge uses a 3-way merge, does rebase also use a 3-way merge?

Yes, often. When rebasing a commit, Git can apply it as a patch, but for conflict resolution it effectively uses a 3-way merge: - base: the original parent of the commit being replayed - ours: the current state of the rebased branch (new base plus previously applied commits) - theirs: the commit’s snapshot (or its changes) This is why conflict markers look similar. Rebase isn’t just “apply diff blindly”; it can do a 3-way merge to reduce conflicts.
Is there a way to preview what a rebase will do before actually doing it?

You can approximate by inspecting the commit range that would be replayed: - git log --oneline --graph main..feature These are the commits that would be replayed if you rebase feature onto main. You can also do a dry run-like check by attempting: - git rebase --onto main <upstream> --no-rebase-merges But Git doesn’t have a perfect “dry run” mode that guarantees no changes; rebase is an operation. A safer approach is: - create a temporary branch pointer: - git branch feature-backup - then rebase feature If you don’t like it, you can reset feature back to feature-backup. Or do the rebase on a new branch: - git switch -c feature-rebased - git rebase main leaving original feature untouched.
That backup branch trick seems handy. Does it cost anything?

It’s cheap. A branch is just a pointer to a commit. Creating it is basically instantaneous and small. - git branch backup-before-rebase Now you have a name referencing the old tip. Even if you rewrite feature, you can always recover by resetting to that backup.
What about stash vs backup branch? When do I use which?

They solve different problems: - Branch backup preserves a commit history state (the tip). It’s about commits and refs. - Stash preserves uncommitted working tree and index changes. It’s about your working directory state. Before rebase, you might: - stash uncommitted work (or commit it), - and optionally create a backup branch to preserve pre-rebase commit pointer.

I think I’m ready to summarize. Let me try: Use merge to combine branches without rewriting; use rebase to move commits onto a new base for a linear history, but avoid rebasing commits that are already pushed unless you’re okay with force-pushing. Is that accurate?

Yes. I’d refine it slightly: - Merge: preserves existing commits; may add a merge commit; good when you want to keep topology and avoid rewriting shared history. - Rebase: rewrites commits by replaying them; good for cleaning up and keeping topic branches current; safest on local/unshared commits; if used on pushed branches, it implies force-push and can complicate other clones. That’s the core.
Can you give me a “decision tree” style checklist?

Sure: 1) Do you need to keep existing commit hashes stable? - Yes → prefer merge (or don’t rewrite). - No → rebase is possible. 2) Are the commits you would rewrite already pushed somewhere you care about? - Yes → avoid rebase, or be prepared to force-push and handle divergence. - No → rebase is generally fine. 3) Do you want a linear history on the target branch? - Yes → rebase + fast-forward merge, or squash merge. - No / you want explicit integration points → merge (possibly --no-ff). 4) Do you want to preserve individual commits as-is? - Yes → merge or rebase (but rebase rewrites hashes). - No, you want one commit → squash. 5) Is the branch history complex with merges you want to preserve? - Yes → merge, or rebase --rebase-merges (advanced). - No → normal rebase is fine.
That’s helpful. One last thing: could you show a realistic session transcript of updating a feature branch with rebase and then integrating it?

Example session: 1) You’re on feature: - git switch feature 2) Update remote-tracking refs: - git fetch origin 3) Rebase feature onto latest main: - git rebase origin/main If conflicts: - git status - resolve conflicts in files - git add <resolved files> - git rebase --continue Repeat until done. 4) Run tests/build if you have them. 5) If feature was never pushed or you don’t care about rewriting remote: - git push origin feature If feature was pushed and you rebased it: - git push --force-with-lease origin feature 6) Integrate into main locally (if you want fast-forward): - git switch main - git fetch origin - git reset --hard origin/main  (optional if you want local main exact) - git merge --ff-only feature 7) Push updated main: - git push origin main That’s the “rebase then fast-forward merge” path.
And the merge-based session would look like?

Merge-based update + integrate: Update feature with main changes (without rewriting feature): - git switch feature - git fetch origin - git merge origin/main Resolve conflicts if any, commit the merge (Git does it automatically once resolved and added). Push feature: - git push origin feature Integrate feature into main (creating merge commit if not FF): - git switch main - git fetch origin - git merge feature   (or origin/feature) - git push origin main If you want to force a merge commit even if FF: - git merge --no-ff feature
Great. I think I’m set. But I’m sure I’ll forget. If you had to give me just one sentence to remember, what would it be?
Merge adds history; rebase rewrites history—so rebase freely on local commits to keep things tidy, and merge when you need stable shared history or explicit integration points.
My app has about 20 components that need shared state. Is Redux overkill?

It depends on what “shared state” means and how often it changes, but for a medium-sized app Redux can be either a good fit or unnecessary ceremony. A quick decision lens: - If the shared state is mostly UI state (open/close, selected tab, filters) and you don’t need sophisticated debugging, Redux might be overkill. - If the shared state is business-critical (auth/session, cart/checkout, multi-step workflows), used across many routes, and you care about predictable updates, time-travel debugging, and strict patterns, Redux (specifically Redux Toolkit) can be worth it. - Zustand is often a sweet spot for “global state without ceremony”: minimal boilerplate, easy selectors, good performance. - React Context is fine for low-frequency state (theme, locale, auth user object) but can become painful for frequently-updating state because it can trigger wide re-renders unless carefully structured. To answer more concretely: what kinds of state are shared across those ~20 components (server data cache, form data, UI flags, derived data)? And do you already use something like React Query for server state?
Shared state includes: authenticated user info, feature flags, a few global UI things (modals, toast queue), and a “builder” workflow state used across 3 pages. Server data is handled by React Query.

Given that breakdown, Redux is not automatically overkill, but you likely don’t need Redux for server data (React Query covers that). For your list: - Authenticated user info: could be Context or Zustand. If it changes rarely, Context is fine. - Feature flags: also rarely changes; Context is fine. - Global UI (modals/toasts): Zustand tends to be very ergonomic; Redux also works. - Builder workflow state across 3 pages: this is the most “app state” part; Zustand or Redux Toolkit both fit well. Context can work but can get clunky if the workflow state is complex and updated frequently. A pragmatic approach for medium apps using React Query is: - React Query for server state - Zustand for client state that changes often (UI + workflow) - Context for truly static-ish “ambient” values (theme/locale) or where you want provider scoping If you want one tool for everything client-side: Redux Toolkit is fine, but you’ll write more scaffolding (slices, selectors, dispatch) than Zustand. How complex is the builder workflow state? Is it like a big nested object with many actions, undo/redo, validation, derived steps?

Builder state is a nested object with about 30 fields, some arrays, and a few derived values. No undo/redo right now, but there’s validation and step gating. Updates happen frequently as the user edits.

That’s the kind of state where Context can become awkward because frequent updates can cause unnecessary re-renders unless you split contexts or use context selectors (not built-in). Redux Toolkit or Zustand are both strong here. Trade-offs: 1) Redux Toolkit: - Pros: very explicit actions, predictable updates, excellent devtools, easy to add middleware, good patterns for validation/derived state, strong ecosystem. - Cons: more boilerplate, more indirection (dispatch actions), mental overhead. 2) Zustand: - Pros: minimal boilerplate, direct set/get, easy to colocate actions with state, good performance with selectors, less ceremony for forms/workflows. - Cons: fewer enforced patterns; you need discipline for structure/testing. Devtools exist but not as standardized as Redux; still good though. Given your workflow has frequent updates and nested data, I’d lean Zustand unless you specifically want Redux’s strictness and devtools for complex debugging. A typical Zustand shape: - A store for builder state with actions like setField, addItem, removeItem, validateStep. - Use selectors in components so only the necessary pieces re-render. Question: do you anticipate needing to persist the builder state (localStorage), sync it to URL, or share it across browser tabs?
Persist to localStorage would be nice. URL sync maybe for a couple fields, but not everything. No cross-tab sync.

Zustand makes persistence straightforward via middleware (persist). Redux can also persist (redux-persist), but it’s a bit heavier. A practical architecture: - Builder store in Zustand with persist: - Persist only the parts that matter (partialize) to avoid storing transient errors. - Versioning/migrations for schema changes. - For URL sync: - Keep URL as a projection: read initial values from URL on load, then write back only the few fields you care about. - Don’t try to store the whole builder in the URL. If you’re worried about discipline/structure with Zustand, you can still enforce patterns: - Use TypeScript types for state and actions. - Keep actions pure-ish (compute next state based on inputs). - Put validation logic in dedicated functions or a “domain” module. Do you already use TypeScript, and do you prefer immutable update patterns or “mutate draft” style?
TypeScript yes. I prefer immutable patterns but I’m fine with Immer style too if it simplifies nested updates.

Then both Redux Toolkit and Zustand-with-Immer are comfortable. Redux Toolkit uses Immer by default in createSlice, which is great for nested updates. Zustand can use an immer middleware or just do immutable updates manually. Decision heuristic: - Choose Redux Toolkit if you want: - Strong conventions for large teams - Very consistent debugging with Redux DevTools - Clear action log and traceability - Choose Zustand if you want: - Less boilerplate - Store-as-a-hook ergonomics - Easy selective subscriptions and small mental footprint With ~20 components and a builder workflow, I’d implement: - Context for auth + flags (rare updates) - Zustand for builder + UI (toasts/modals) But if you want fewer primitives, you can put auth/flags in Zustand too; it’s fine. Do you have multiple routes needing different instances of the builder (e.g., multiple builders open), or is it singleton?
Singleton. Just one builder at a time.

That simplifies things: a single global store is fine. A concrete Zustand store sketch (TypeScript): - State: - builder: fields, arrays - validation: errors per field/step - ui: currentStep, isDirty - Actions: - setField(path, value) or more explicit setName, setFoo - addItem/removeItem/updateItem - validate() or validateStep(step) - reset(), loadFromDraft() Use selectors like: - const fieldA = useBuilderStore(s => s.builder.fieldA) - const setFieldA = useBuilderStore(s => s.setFieldA) This avoids re-rendering components that don’t depend on changing parts. If you use Redux, you’d do a slice with reducers and selectors. It’s more files but very standardized. What’s your biggest concern: performance, maintainability, or onboarding?
Maintainability and avoiding a mess. Performance second.

For maintainability, the risk with Zustand is not the library; it’s letting the store become a “dumping ground” without conventions. You can mitigate that by adopting some structure: - Split stores by domain: builderStore, uiStore, sessionStore (even if all created with Zustand). - Keep actions grouped and named consistently. - Avoid arbitrary setState from components; expose actions that represent intent (e.g., setShippingAddress, addRule) rather than generic set(path). - Write selectors and reuse them; don’t inline complex selectors everywhere. - Keep derived values out of state when possible; compute them via selectors or helper functions. Redux Toolkit enforces some of this by design (actions/reducers). If you value guardrails, Redux Toolkit may be the safer bet. But since you’re medium-sized and want pragmatism, Zustand with conventions can be very maintainable. A compromise approach: - Use Zustand, but treat it like “mini Redux”: - Define an explicit action interface - Keep state updates centralized - Add devtools middleware - Add persist with migrations Do you need to support unit testing of state transitions (given initial state + action => next state)?
Yes, I’d like to test the builder logic and validation.

Then structure matters more than the library. Two good patterns: 1) “Pure domain functions” approach: - Put builder transitions and validation in pure functions: - updateField(state, payload) => newState - validate(state) => errors - Store actions call these functions. - Tests target the pure functions, independent of Zustand/Redux. 2) Redux-style reducer approach even in Zustand: - Implement a reducer function (state, action) => state and dispatch actions into it. - Zustand supports a “redux” middleware, or you can roll your own dispatch. For most maintainable testing, I’d do (1): pure functions + thin store actions. Example: - domain/builder.ts: - export function setField(builder, field, value): Builder - export function computeStepStatus(builder): StepStatus - export function validateBuilder(builder): ValidationErrors Then Zustand store: - setField: (field, value) => set(s => ({ builder: setField(s.builder, field, value), errors: validateBuilder(...) })) Redux store: - reducer uses those same pure functions. This reduces lock-in and keeps tests stable. Do you want validation to run on every keystroke, or only on blur/step submit?
Both. Basic validation on change, deeper validation on step submit.

That maps well to two layers: - Lightweight “onChange” validation: - e.g., required fields, simple format checks - cheap enough to run frequently - Heavy “onSubmit/step” validation: - cross-field rules, array constraints, maybe async checks Implementation idea: - Store only the minimal validation results in state (errors + touched). - For onChange: - Update field - Run validateField(field, builder) and update that field’s error - For onStepSubmit: - Run validateStep(step, builder) returning errors for step fields - Set errors + mark touched for those fields - Gate navigation based on error presence Either Zustand or Redux can do this. Zustand might be slightly simpler because actions can access get() to read current builder state. One caveat: avoid doing expensive derived computations in every component render. Prefer selectors or memoization. Are you planning to use a form library (React Hook Form, Formik), or is the builder custom?
It’s custom; some fields are dynamic arrays and a few custom widgets. I might still use React Hook Form, but not sure.

If you use React Hook Form (RHF), it can reduce global state needs because RHF manages form state efficiently with uncontrolled inputs. Then your global store could hold only: - the persisted “draft” snapshot - step navigation state - non-form UI state (toasts/modals) But with dynamic arrays and custom widgets, RHF still works; it just requires Controller wrappers and careful field array usage. If you don’t use RHF, then your store becomes the form state manager. In that case: - Zustand is a nice fit for form-like state because subscriptions are granular. - Redux can also do it, but you’ll dispatch on every keystroke; it’s fine, but might feel heavy. If you do RHF, Redux/Zustand might be used for: - wizard step state - saving/loading drafts - side effects Do you want the builder state to be accessible outside the builder pages (e.g., header summary, preview panel)?
Yes, there’s a persistent preview panel and a header summary that shows key fields.

Then a global store is appropriate, or you can scope a provider around the entire builder area including header/preview. If the builder is only active on certain routes, scoping is beneficial: - You can mount a provider when entering builder routes and unmount on exit, auto-resetting state. - With Zustand, you can create a store instance per provider using vanilla store + context pattern. - With Redux, you can mount a store provider similarly, but usually apps have one global store. Given singleton builder and preview/header, I’d likely scope it to the builder section to avoid stale state elsewhere, unless other parts of the app truly need it. Do you prefer a single global store for everything, or are you okay with a “builder module” that has its own store/provider?
I’m okay with a builder-specific provider if it helps avoid global pollution.

Then Context becomes more viable as a wrapper—but the frequent updates are still the issue. The better approach is: - Create a builder store (Zustand vanilla store) - Provide it via React Context (just to pass the store instance) - Components use a hook like useBuilderStore(selector) that subscribes to the vanilla store with selector support This gives you: - Scoped lifetime (mount/unmount) - Avoids Context re-render problem because Context value is stable (store instance) - Granular subscriptions for performance - Testability and modularity This is a common pattern for Zustand in larger apps. Redux can also be scoped, but it’s less common to have multiple Redux stores; still possible. Do you have SSR requirements (Next.js SSR) where initial state hydration matters?
It’s a Next.js app with SSR, but the builder is mostly client-only. Auth is server-aware.

For Next.js: - Redux has established patterns for SSR/hydration, but it’s extra work. - Zustand also supports SSR; the main caution is not to share store instances between requests on the server. If builder is client-only, it’s simpler. Auth being server-aware suggests: - Use Next.js server components or server-side session fetching, then pass user down to client. - For client state, avoid duplicating server session state unnecessarily. One pragmatic setup: - Auth/user from server (e.g., via next-auth or custom) -> client provider with stable user object. - Feature flags fetched server-side or via React Query; then store in Context. - Builder store is client-only and persisted to localStorage; only initialize after mount to avoid hydration mismatch. If you persist builder to localStorage, you’ll need to handle “rehydration” carefully so SSR markup doesn’t differ. Typically you: - Render builder UI only after hydration, or - Use a “hasHydrated” flag from persist middleware and show skeleton until rehydrated. Is hydration mismatch a concern for your builder routes?
Yes, I’ve run into hydration issues before when reading localStorage too early.

Then you’ll want a clear hydration strategy: - Don’t read localStorage during SSR. - Initialize store with default state on server render. - On client, rehydrate from localStorage after mount. - Gate rendering of parts that depend on persisted state until rehydration completes. In Zustand persist, there’s an onRehydrateStorage callback and a hasHydrated flag you can implement. Alternatively, keep persisted draft separate: - On mount, load draft async, then dispatch “loadDraft(draft)” into store. - Until loaded, show skeleton. Redux has similar patterns but more plumbing. Given this, Zustand with persist + hydration flag is straightforward. Do you want Redux DevTools-style action logging, or is that not important?
It’s helpful, but not mandatory.

Zustand can integrate with Redux DevTools enough to be useful (naming actions), but Redux is still the gold standard for structured logs. If you choose Zustand, do: - devtools middleware - name actions (e.g., set(..., false, 'builder/setField')) That gives you a readable timeline. For maintainability, also consider: - Use selectors and shallow comparison to avoid re-renders. - Keep store minimal; let React Query handle server state. - Avoid storing derived values unless necessary; compute in selectors. At this point, your requirements align well with: - Zustand (scoped provider) for builder + UI state - Context for auth/flags (or also Zustand if you want one pattern) Do you want a single approach across the app to reduce cognitive load, or is mixing Context+Zustand okay?
I’d prefer fewer patterns. Maybe one primary state approach for client state.

Then I’d pick Zustand for all client-side shared state (auth snapshot, flags, UI, builder), while still using React Query for server state. That gives you one mental model: “if multiple components need it and it’s client state, it goes in Zustand.” Caveat: auth is often better treated as server state or session context; but you can store the client-facing user object in Zustand as a read-only snapshot and update it on login/logout events. So: - React Query: server cache - Zustand: client state - Minimal Context: only for providing scoped store instances (builder), or not needed if global. If you want scoping for builder but still “one approach,” you can still say “Zustand everywhere,” just sometimes scoped. Would you like a concrete store structure proposal (files/modules) for this app?
Yes. Propose a folder structure and how many stores you’d create.
