A pragmatic structure: src/ state/ session/ sessionStore.ts sessionTypes.ts flags/ flagsStore.ts flagsTypes.ts ui/ uiStore.ts toastTypes.ts modalTypes.ts builder/ builderStore.ts builderTypes.ts builderDomain.ts builderValidation.ts builderSelectors.ts BuilderStoreProvider.tsx  (only if scoped) api/ (React Query hooks) components/ pages/ or app/ Store count: - sessionStore: user, auth status, maybe token if applicable (avoid storing sensitive tokens if you can) - flagsStore: feature flags (or merge into sessionStore if flags are tied to user/org) - uiStore: toasts/modals/global UI - builderStore: scoped to builder routes if you want; otherwise global If you truly want fewer, you can combine session+flags into “appStore” and keep ui+builder separate: - appStore (session + flags) - uiStore - builderStore (scoped) I’d avoid one giant store for everything; splitting by domain improves maintainability without adding much overhead. Each store exports: - useXStore hook - selectors (either functions or exported hooks) - actions builderDomain.ts contains pure functions for transitions and validation; builderStore calls them. Do you want a code-level example of a builderStore with persist + devtools + hydration flag?
Yes, show how you’d do persist, devtools, and a hasHydrated flag, plus a couple actions.

You can implement it with Zustand’s persist + devtools, and add a small hydration flag. Rough outline: - State: - builder: Builder - errors: ValidationErrors - currentStep: number - hasHydrated: boolean - Actions: - setField(field, value) - addItem(item) - validateStep(step): boolean - reset() Persist: - partialize to store builder + currentStep (maybe), not errors - version + migrate Hydration: - set hasHydrated true in onRehydrateStorage callback Also, name actions for devtools. Do you want the store to be scoped (created per provider) or global singleton? The code differs slightly.
Scoped per provider for the builder.

Then use a vanilla store factory and a provider: - createBuilderStore(initialState?) returns a Zustand vanilla store - BuilderStoreProvider creates the store once (useRef) and provides it via context - useBuilderStore(selector) reads store from context and uses zustand’s useStore to subscribe This avoids Context re-render issues and gives scoping. Do you want the provider to accept initial values from server/URL?
Yes, initial values from URL for a couple fields.

Then BuilderStoreProvider can take an initialBuilderPatch parsed from URL and merge it into default state before creating the store. Persist rehydration will then override as needed (depending on your merge strategy). You’ll need to decide precedence: Common precedence: - If a persisted draft exists, it wins. - URL patch can override certain fields even on top of persisted (if you want shareable links). Implement by: - Default state + URL patch at creation - On rehydrate, in migrate or onRehydrate, merge persisted builder with URL patch for whitelisted fields. This is subtle; you don’t want the URL to accidentally wipe a user’s draft. If you want, I can outline a deterministic precedence function.
Yes, define a precedence rule and how you’d implement it.

A clear rule: 1) Start with defaults. 2) Apply persisted draft (if any). 3) Apply URL overrides for a small whitelist of fields (e.g., templateId, mode). 4) Recompute derived fields and clear transient validation errors. Implementation approach: - Store a “urlOverrides” object in memory (not persisted) passed to provider. - In persist’s merge option (if available) or in onRehydrateStorage: - Take persisted state - Apply overrides: next.builder = applyOverrides(persisted.builder, urlOverrides) - Set state with next + hasHydrated true - Ensure partialize excludes hasHydrated, errors, urlOverrides. If persist middleware doesn’t give you the exact hooks you want, you can: - Disable automatic merge (or accept it) - Then in onRehydrateStorage, call store.setState to apply overrides after hydration. Key is to keep overrides explicit and limited. Would you like to discuss when Redux would still be preferable here, so you’re confident in the choice?
Yes. Give me the cases where Redux Toolkit would be the better decision.

Redux Toolkit is likely better if: - You expect the app to grow significantly (many domains, many contributors) and you want strong conventions enforced by the tooling. - You need sophisticated debugging: - action-by-action timeline - ability to replay issues from logs - consistent action shapes across the app - You rely on middleware patterns: - centralized side effects (thunks/sagas/observables) - logging, analytics, error reporting tied to actions - You have complex derived state and want memoized selectors (Reselect) as a first-class pattern. - You want to integrate with an ecosystem that assumes Redux (some enterprise tooling, though less common now). - You prefer reducer-centric testing: pure reducers + actions. In your case, since server state is React Query and builder is a complex client workflow, Redux is not wrong—just heavier. If your team strongly prefers explicit action logs and strict patterns, Redux Toolkit may reduce long-term entropy. If the main goal is “avoid a mess,” Redux’s discipline is a real advantage, but you can replicate a lot of it with conventions in Zustand.
How does React Context compare if I tried to do this with multiple contexts and memoization?

You can make Context work, but it’s usually more effort than it seems for frequently changing state. Problems: - When a context value changes, all consumers re-render, even if they only use part of the value. - You can split into many contexts (one per field group) to reduce blast radius, but then you get provider nesting and complexity. - You can memoize context values, but if the value includes changing state, the reference changes anyway. - There are “context selector” libraries (use-context-selector) that mitigate this, but then you’re essentially adding a state management library. If your builder updates on keystrokes, Context will either: - cause too many re-renders, or - require architectural contortions (many contexts, selectors lib, heavy memoization) That’s why Zustand/Redux are better for this use-case: they provide subscription granularity. Context is still great for: - stable configuration (theme, locale) - services (logger, API client) - session object that changes rarely
If I go with Zustand, how do I prevent components from re-rendering too often?

The big levers: 1) Use selectors that pick only what the component needs: - Bad: useStore(s => s.builder) (re-renders on any builder change) - Better: useStore(s => s.builder.title) - Better for multiple fields: useStore(s => ({ title: s.builder.title, mode: s.builder.mode }), shallow) 2) Use shallow comparison for object/array selections: - Zustand provides shallow comparator (or you can write one). - Without shallow, selecting an object literal causes re-render every time. 3) Keep state normalized where it helps: - Large arrays of items: store by id + list of ids so updates don’t replace whole arrays unnecessarily. - Or update arrays immutably but carefully. 4) Avoid storing derived values that change often unless necessary: - Derived values can be computed in selectors, memoized if needed. 5) Use React memo for presentational components, but fix the store subscriptions first. Also, avoid passing huge slices as props down the tree; let leaf components subscribe directly to the store.
What about using Zustand with immer? Any pitfalls?

It’s convenient for nested updates, but watch for: - Performance: Immer has overhead, but usually fine for medium apps. Hot paths with very frequent updates on large objects might feel it; measure if concerned. - Accidental mutation outside Immer: if you expose state objects and mutate them elsewhere, you can break expectations. Keep state encapsulated; don’t let consumers mutate arrays/objects directly. - Equality: even with Immer, updates replace parent references along the modified path, which is good. But if you update large objects often, many selectors might re-render if they select high-level objects. A balanced approach: - Use Immer for complex nested updates (builder fields). - Still keep selectors narrow to minimize re-renders.
How do I organize actions? One action per field seems verbose; a generic setField seems too loose.

Common middle ground: - Provide a small set of “intentful” actions for major operations: - setName, setMode, setTemplateId (for key fields) - addItem, removeItem, updateItemQuantity - setStep, nextStep, prevStep - For the long tail of simple scalar fields, use a typed generic setter: - setField<K extends keyof Builder>(key: K, value: Builder[K]) This keeps type safety without 30 bespoke functions. For nested fields, you can: - create specific actions for nested structures (e.g., setAddressField) - or define small sub-objects and typed setters for them Avoid a string-path setter like setField('a.b.c', value) unless you really need it; it tends to bypass type safety and can hide complexity.
How would Redux Toolkit look for the same builder? What would be the boilerplate difference?

Redux Toolkit version typically includes: - builderSlice.ts: - initialState - reducers: setField, addItem, removeItem, validateStep, reset - extraReducers if async - selectors: - selectBuilder, selectCurrentStep, selectErrors, selectIsValid - store.ts to configure store (if not already) - Provider at app root (or scoped) - useAppDispatch/useAppSelector hooks Boilerplate difference: - Redux requires explicit dispatch and action creators, though RTK generates them. - Zustand is mostly a store definition and direct hooks. If you already have Redux in the app, adding a slice is easy. If you don’t, setting it up for just one workflow may feel heavier.
I don’t have Redux right now. I’m leaning Zustand. But I’m worried about debugging and accidental complexity later.

Then bake in a few guardrails from day one: - Enable devtools middleware; name actions consistently. - Enforce action-only updates: - Don’t export setState; export only actions and selectors. - Add lint rules/conventions: - No component should call set with arbitrary partial state; only through actions. - Keep domain logic in pure functions and test them. - Split stores by domain. - Document store boundaries: what belongs in builderStore vs uiStore, etc. Also, create a small “state design doc”: - what goes in React Query vs store - persistence rules - URL sync rules - how to add new state That prevents the store from becoming a dumping ground.
How should I handle toasts and modals? A queue for toasts, and modals can stack.

A uiStore is perfect for this. Toasts: - state: toasts: Array<{id, type, message, createdAt, ...}> - actions: - pushToast(toast) - removeToast(id) - clearToasts() - optionally auto-expire: - either in the component using setTimeout and calling removeToast - or in store with a timer map (I usually keep timers outside store to keep it pure) Modals stacking: - state: modalStack: Array<{id, type, props}> - actions: - openModal(type, props) => returns id - closeModal(id) or closeTop() - replaceModal(...) - Consider focus management and scroll lock in UI layer (component), not store. Persist? Usually no. Selectors: - useUiStore(s => s.modalStack[s.modalStack.length - 1]) for top modal - Or render all stacked modals.
If I use separate stores, can actions coordinate across stores? For example, on logout clear builder and ui.

Yes. Options: 1) “Orchestrator” function: - A logout handler calls: - sessionStore.getState().logout() - builderStore.getState().reset() - uiStore.getState().clearToasts() This keeps cross-store coordination outside the stores, which is clean. 2) Cross-store import: - sessionStore logout action imports builderStore and uiStore and resets them. This couples stores and can create circular dependencies if not careful. I prefer (1): keep orchestration in a service layer (e.g., src/state/actions/logout.ts) or in the auth module. If you scope builder store to builder routes, logout might just navigate away and unmount the provider, which resets automatically.
What about feature flags? They come from the server and might update when user changes org.

Treat flags as server-derived config: - If they come from React Query, you can keep them there and read via a hook. - If you want easy access without threading query hooks everywhere, store a snapshot in a flagsStore: - actions: setFlags(flags), clearFlags() - selectors: isEnabled(flagKey) But avoid duplicating too much; keep a single source of truth. If flags depend on org changes: - On org switch event: - invalidate flags query - update flags store when new flags arrive - Or just read from query in components that need it. If flags rarely change and you want minimal patterns, a flagsStore is fine.
I’ve heard Redux is better for normalized data. But I’m using React Query for server data. Is normalization still relevant?

Normalization is mainly for large relational datasets and updates across many places. React Query typically stores data per query key; it’s not a normalized entity cache by default (though you can structure it that way). Since you’re using React Query: - If you need true normalized caching with fine-grained updates, you might consider something else (Apollo for GraphQL, RTK Query with entity adapters, or custom normalization). - But for many apps, React Query is enough, and normalization isn’t required. For your builder state (client-only), normalization can still help if you have arrays of complex items that you update frequently and you want stable references. But don’t normalize prematurely; do it if performance or update complexity demands it.
Let’s talk about selectors. How do I avoid writing tons of selector functions?

You don’t need a huge selector library, but a few patterns help: - For simple fields, inline selectors are fine: - useBuilderStore(s => s.builder.title) - For repeated derived logic, extract selectors: - const selectIsStepValid = (step) => (s) => ... - Or export hooks: useIsStepValid(step) - For expensive derived computations, memoize: - In Redux you’d use Reselect. - In Zustand, you can: - compute derived values in a hook with useMemo based on selected primitives, or - keep derived caches in store (careful), or - use a small memoization helper keyed by inputs. A pragmatic approach: - Keep selector extraction for: - step gating logic - summary computations used in many places - derived lists (filtered items)
If I compute derived values in components, won’t that cause repeated work?

It can, but you can limit it: - Select only the primitive inputs needed for the derived computation. - Use useMemo with those inputs. - Ensure the inputs are stable (avoid selecting huge objects). Example: - Select itemIds and itemsById references. - Derived: compute total price. useMemo recomputes only when those references change. If derived logic is shared across many components, move it into a selector module and reuse it, still driven by primitive selections.
How would you handle step gating logic and navigation?

Keep step state and gating rules explicit: - State: - currentStep - completedSteps: boolean[] or Set<number> - touched fields per step (optional) - Actions: - goToStep(step) - nextStep() - prevStep() - completeStep(step) - Gating: - canEnterStep(step, builder, errors, completedSteps) - canAdvanceFromStep(currentStep, builder, errors) On “Next”: - validateStep(currentStep) - if ok: mark completed, increment step On direct navigation: - allow going back always - allow forward only if prerequisites completed and current step valid (depending on UX) Store should own the canonical currentStep. UI components call actions.
Suppose I later need undo/redo in the builder. Does that push me to Redux?

Not necessarily. Undo/redo can be implemented in Zustand or Redux. Redux has established patterns (redux-undo) and devtools synergy, but Zustand can do it with a history stack: - State: - past: Builder[] - present: Builder - future: Builder[] - Actions: - applyChange(fn) pushes present to past, updates present, clears future - undo(), redo() Pitfalls: - Memory usage if builder state is big and changes often. You can: - limit history length - store patches instead of full snapshots (more complex) - debounce history entries (only commit after pause) If undo/redo becomes important, Redux might feel more “standard,” but Zustand can handle it fine.
Another concern: concurrency with React 18. Any issues with Zustand and concurrent rendering?

Zustand is designed to work with React 18 via useSyncExternalStore under the hood (in recent versions). As long as you’re on a modern Zustand version, it should be concurrency-safe. General guidance: - Avoid reading store state outside React in ways that assume synchronous rendering. - Prefer selectors in components. - For actions, it’s fine to call get() and set(). Redux also uses useSyncExternalStore in react-redux v8, so both are fine.
How do I integrate Zustand with React Query? For example, when a query returns user data, update session store.

Two patterns: 1) “Derived from query” (no duplication): - Components call useUserQuery() - Pass data down or use it directly - Session store only holds auth tokens or minimal client flags This avoids duplication but can be repetitive. 2) “Snapshot into store”: - In a top-level effect (e.g., in a SessionProvider component), subscribe to query results and set session store: - useEffect(() => { if (user) setUser(user) }, [user]) This makes user available everywhere via store. Be careful: - Don’t fight React Query. If you store the user snapshot, still consider React Query the source of truth and update store from it. - Handle loading/undefined states clearly.
What about SSR for session: user is available server-side. How do I initialize the client store?

You can pass initial session data from server to client as props (or via Next.js app router patterns) and initialize session store on the client: - In a client component at the top of your layout: - sessionStore.setState({ user: initialUser, status: 'authenticated' }, false, 'session/init') Or if you want scoping, create a provider with initial state. Make sure you don’t re-initialize on every render: - useRef to ensure it runs once - Or check a “initialized” flag in store Also consider hydration mismatch: - If server rendered with user info, the client should start with the same user info.
I’m using Next.js app router. Some components are server, some client. Any gotchas?

Main gotchas: - Zustand store lives in client land. Server components can’t read it. - If a server component needs session/flags, fetch them on the server and pass as props to client components. - Avoid importing client-only modules (that reference window/localStorage) into server components. - For persisted builder state, ensure builder routes are client components or contain client subtrees where the store is used. So: - Server components handle data fetching and pass initial data down. - Client components handle interactive state via Zustand/React Query.
Can I keep the builder store entirely client-side and still have server-rendered shell?

Yes. Typical approach: - Server-render the layout and non-interactive parts. - The builder page includes a client component that mounts the builder provider and renders the interactive UI. - Until hydration, render a placeholder/skeleton. This avoids localStorage issues during SSR.
Let’s revisit Redux one more time: if I choose Redux Toolkit, would you advise RTK Query instead of React Query?

If you’re going Redux, RTK Query is a strong option for server state and integrates nicely with Redux devtools and caching. But switching from React Query is not required. Comparisons: - React Query is library-agnostic, very mature, great DX. - RTK Query is excellent but ties server cache to Redux store. If you already have React Query and like it, keep it. It’s common to use Redux Toolkit for client state and React Query for server state. Some people prefer “one stack” (Redux + RTK Query), but it’s not mandatory.
If I keep React Query and add Redux for client state, any duplication concerns?

Just be disciplined about boundaries: - React Query: anything fetched from server, including caching, loading/error, background refetch, pagination. - Redux: client-only state and UI/workflow state. Avoid storing server responses in Redux “just because.” That leads to duplication, stale copies, and extra synchronization code.
For Zustand, how do I handle async actions? For example, saving builder draft to server.

You can write async actions directly: - saveDraft: async () => { const builder = get().builder set({ saving: true }) try { await api.save(builder); set({ saving: false, lastSavedAt: ... }) } catch (e) { set({ saving: false, saveError: ... }) } } Or use React Query mutations for server interactions: - useSaveDraftMutation() - On success, update store state (e.g., lastSavedAt) and maybe clear dirty flag. Given you already use React Query, I’d use mutations for saving: - It handles retries, cache invalidation, error states. - Store holds only local flags like isDirty, lastSavedAt, and the builder data.
Should isDirty be derived or stored?

Usually derived: - isDirty = builder !== lastSavedSnapshot (or compare fields) But deep comparisons are expensive. Pragmatic approach: - Store isDirty boolean and set it to true on any user-initiated change. - Set it to false when you load draft or after successful save (and perhaps store a savedVersion). If you need more precision (e.g., revert changes), you can store a baseline snapshot and compare selectively.
How do I avoid marking dirty when I apply URL overrides or rehydrate?

Distinguish between “system” updates and “user” updates: - Actions can accept a meta flag: - setField(key, value, { source: 'user' | 'system' }) - Only set isDirty when source is 'user'. Or separate actions: - hydrateFromPersist(draft) - applyUrlOverrides(overrides) - userSetField(...) This keeps logic explicit and avoids subtle bugs.
What about logging/analytics: track events when certain actions happen.

Options: - In Zustand actions, call an analytics function: - setField: ...; analytics.track('builder_field_changed', { field }) This is simple but mixes concerns. - Wrap store actions: - Create a middleware that intercepts set calls with action names (devtools naming) and forwards to analytics. Zustand doesn’t have as standardized middleware chain as Redux, but you can implement a custom wrapper around set. - Keep analytics in UI handlers: - When user clicks “Next”, track event, then call nextStep() This keeps store pure but may duplicate logic if many entry points. If you want maintainability, I’d track at “semantic” events: - step advanced - draft saved - publish clicked rather than every field change.
Let’s say I implement builder store with a provider. How do I access it in nested components without prop drilling?

The provider pattern solves that: - BuilderStoreProvider at builder route root. - Any nested client component calls useBuilderStore(selector). No prop drilling required. Just ensure nested components are within the provider subtree.
What about having multiple builder instances in tests? Is scoping helpful there?

Yes. Scoping makes tests cleaner: - Render component under BuilderStoreProvider with initial state. - Each test gets a fresh store instance. - No leakage between tests. You can also create the store directly in tests (without React) and test actions/selectors.
How would you test pure domain logic vs store actions?

Recommended split: 1) Domain tests (most of them): - Given a Builder object and an operation, assert next Builder and validation output. - No React, no Zustand. 2) Store tests (a few): - Ensure actions call domain functions correctly and update flags (isDirty, currentStep). - Ensure persistence partialize excludes errors. - Ensure hydration logic sets hasHydrated. 3) Component tests (integration): - Render UI, simulate typing, ensure preview updates, step gating works. This keeps tests stable and fast.
I’m still uneasy about “store as dumping ground.” Give me rules for what goes into builder store vs component local state.

Rules of thumb: Put in builder store: - Data that must be shared across multiple components (preview, header, step pages) - Data that must survive navigation within builder routes - Data that needs persistence (draft) - State that drives gating/navigation globally Keep in component local state: - Transient UI state that doesn’t affect other components: - input focus, local open/close of a dropdown - temporary text in a search box that doesn’t affect preview - optimistic UI for a single component if not shared - Expensive derived view-only state that can be computed locally If a piece of state is only used in one component and doesn’t need to persist or be reflected elsewhere, keep it local.
How do I handle a custom widget that needs intermediate state (like dragging sliders) but only commits on release?

Keep intermediate state local to the widget: - Local state updates on every drag frame. - On release (or debounce), call store action to commit final value. This avoids flooding global store updates and re-rendering preview on every drag tick. If preview must update live, you can still update store but throttle: - requestAnimationFrame throttle or setTimeout debounce. But default to local intermediate state for performance and better UX control.
Can you summarize the recommended approach in one plan?

Plan: - Keep React Query for server state (user fetch, flags fetch, save draft mutation). - Use Zustand for client shared state: - builderStore (scoped provider on builder routes) with persist to localStorage, devtools, hasHydrated, URL overrides. - uiStore (global) for toasts/modals. - sessionStore (global) for client session snapshot if needed. - Put builder transitions and validation in pure domain modules; store actions are thin wrappers. - Use narrow selectors + shallow comparison to control re-renders. - Implement hydration gating to avoid SSR/localStorage mismatches. - Keep component-local transient UI state local; only commit meaningful changes to store.
That sounds good. Now, if I later decide to migrate from Zustand to Redux Toolkit, how hard is it?

If you follow the “domain functions” approach, migration is manageable: - Your pure builderDomain functions stay the same. - You replace Zustand store with a Redux slice that calls those functions in reducers (or uses them to compute next state). - Components that currently call useBuilderStore(selector) will need to switch to useSelector + dispatch, which is a larger refactor. To reduce coupling now: - Create an adapter hook layer: - useBuilderField('title') or useBuilderActions() Then components depend on your adapter API, not Zustand directly. Migration becomes changing adapter internals. If you anticipate possible migration, investing in that adapter layer early can pay off.
I like the adapter idea. What would that look like?

You can expose: - useBuilderValue(selector) or specific hooks: - useBuilderTitle() - useBuilderItems() - useBuilderCurrentStep() - useBuilderActions(): - returns stable references to actions: setField, nextStep, saveDraft, etc. Internally: - For Zustand, these hooks call useBuilderStore with selectors. - For Redux, they call useSelector/useDispatch. This is similar to a “state facade.” Keep it small; don’t over-abstract, just cover common access patterns.
Okay. One last question: if I put auth user in Zustand, how do I prevent stale data when React Query refetches?

Either: - Don’t store user in Zustand; read from React Query wherever needed. Or if you do store it: - Treat Zustand as a view cache: - On query success, setUser(data). - On logout, clearUser. - On refetch, update user. - Also store a userVersion/updatedAt if needed. But you should avoid writing to user store from random components. Centralize it in one place (SessionProvider) that watches the query and updates store. That prevents inconsistent writes.
Thanks. I’ll proceed with Zustand scoped builder store plus ui store. I might keep session in React Query for now.

That’s a solid, pragmatic split. If you want, list the exact builder fields/arrays and step structure, and the heaviest derived computations; then the store shape (normalized vs nested) and selector strategy can be tuned to minimize re-renders and keep updates simple.

Builder has steps: Basics, Items, Rules, Review. Items is an array of up to 200 entries; each entry has id, name, quantity, price, tags. Rules references items by id. Preview shows totals and some derived grouping by tag.

With up to 200 items and derived grouping, be careful about re-rendering and recomputation. Recommendations: State shape: - Normalize items: - itemsById: Record<string, Item> - itemIds: string[] This makes updating one item not require recreating a 200-length array (though you still update itemsById reference). It also helps rules reference ids naturally. Rules: - rules: array referencing item ids; keep them separate. Derived computations: - totals: sum(quantity*price) - groupByTag: tag -> {count, total} Don’t store these derived values in state unless necessary. Instead: - Create selector hooks that compute derived values from inputs. - Memoize derived computations: - Use a selector that depends on stable references (itemsById and itemIds). - When updating a single item, itemsById changes, so memoization based on reference alone will still recompute everything. If that becomes expensive, consider incremental updates or store derived caches. For 200 items, full recompute is usually fine if not on every keystroke across many components. But preview updating on each change could cause noticeable work. Optimization options: - Debounce preview computations (UI-level) if acceptable. - Maintain derived totals incrementally in store: - When updating an item quantity/price, update totals by delta. This is more complex but can be worth it. A hybrid: - Keep totals incrementally (cheap, frequently needed). - Compute grouping by tag on demand with memoization. Also, ensure item row components subscribe only to their item: - useItem(itemId) selector returns that item. - List component subscribes to itemIds only. This minimizes re-renders: updating one item rerenders only its row + preview, not the whole list.
How would you implement useItem(itemId) with a scoped store?

In the adapter layer: - useBuilderStore is available via context. - Define: - function useItem(itemId: string) { return useBuilderStore(s => s.itemsById[itemId]) } - function useUpdateItem() returns action updateItem Then in ItemRow: - const item = useItem(id) - const updateItem = useUpdateItem() - onChange => updateItem(id, patch) Also: - The list component selects itemIds and maps to ItemRow with key=id. ItemRow subscribes to its item; list doesn’t need the whole itemsById.
If itemsById is an object, updating one item changes the object reference. Does that cause all ItemRows to re-render?

Not if each row selects only s.itemsById[itemId]. Zustand’s selector subscription compares the selected value, not the whole itemsById object. When you update a different item, the selected value for a given row stays referentially equal, so it won’t re-render. Caveat: ensure your update logic preserves object references for unchanged items. With immutable updates: - itemsById: { ...itemsById, [id]: updatedItem } Unchanged items keep same references, so rows for other ids don’t re-render. If you accidentally recreate all items (e.g., mapping an array to new objects), you’ll cause widespread re-renders. Normalization helps avoid that.
For tag grouping, if I compute from all items on each update, could that be too slow?

For 200 items, grouping by tag is usually fine unless: - updates happen extremely frequently (like typing in name fields) - grouping is complex - preview is large and re-renders are heavy To reduce load: - Only recompute grouping when relevant fields change (quantity/price/tags), not name. You can implement specialized actions: - updateItemName (doesn’t trigger expensive recompute) - updateItemPricing (does) But then you’re managing complexity. Alternative: debounce preview computation by 100–200ms so rapid typing doesn’t recompute on every keystroke. Or compute grouping in a web worker if it becomes heavy (probably unnecessary here).
If I choose to store totals incrementally, how do I keep it correct?

You need updates to go through actions that can compute deltas: - When updating quantity or price: - old = itemsById[id] - new = { ...old, ...patch } - delta = (new.quantity*new.price) - (old.quantity*old.price) - totals.grandTotal += delta - update itemsById[id] = new When adding/removing items: - add: totals += qty*price - remove: totals -= qty*price Keep these as the only mutation pathways. Test them with unit tests. If tags grouping is also incremental, it’s more complex because tags can be added/removed; you’d update per-tag buckets by delta. Given complexity, I’d start without incremental grouping, measure, then optimize if needed.

If you run into a specific issue while implementing—like hydration order, selector re-render surprises, or persist migrations—bring the code shape and the symptoms, and we can narrow it down quickly.
I keep getting a RecursionError in my tree traversal. Can you help me figure out why?

Yes. A RecursionError in a tree traversal usually means either (1) the “tree” isn’t actually a tree (it contains a cycle), (2) the recursion depth is legitimately too large (very deep chain), or (3) the traversal doesn’t hit a base case due to a bug (e.g., wrong child pointer, default argument aliasing, etc.). Can you paste the traversal code and how your node structure is defined, plus a tiny example of how you build the tree?
Sure, here’s the simplified version. It’s supposed to do a DFS and collect values.

class Node: def __init__(self, val, children=None): self.val = val self.children = children or [] def dfs(node, out=[]): out.append(node.val) for c in node.children: dfs(c, out) return out I build it like: a = Node("a") b = Node("b") c = Node("c") a.children = [b, c] b.children = [a]  # I know this is weird; it’s from a graph-like input print(dfs(a)) This blows up.

The blow-up is expected because b.children includes a, so the structure has a cycle: a -> b -> a -> b -> ... Recursion never terminates because there’s no visited check. Also there’s a separate issue: dfs(node, out=[]) uses a mutable default argument that persists across calls, which can cause confusing behavior even when there’s no cycle. To fix the RecursionError for cyclic structures, add a visited set keyed by node identity (id(node)) or by a unique node ID. Example: def dfs(node, out=None, visited=None): if out is None: out = [] if visited is None: visited = set() if id(node) in visited: return out visited.add(id(node)) out.append(node.val) for c in node.children: dfs(c, out, visited) return out Then dfs(a) would return ["a", "b", "c"] (order depends). If you want to treat it as a graph traversal, you need to decide whether you want to skip already-seen nodes, or include them but stop at a depth limit. Does your real data possibly contain cycles like that?

Please paste the parsing and traversal code and a sample input that triggers it. If it’s nested lists, it might be deep nesting or a bug that accidentally recurses on the same object.

How do I fix it? I tried sys.setrecursionlimit(10000) but it still sometimes fails or feels wrong.

That’s a classic deep recursion case. Your structure is essentially a linked list-like binary tree skewed to one side, depth ~2000, and both build() and inorder() are recursive, so you can hit recursion limits (and even C stack limits) depending on platform. Raising recursionlimit can work up to a point, but it’s brittle. Better: rewrite build and/or traversal iteratively. Option A: Avoid building a node tree at all; traverse the nested list directly iteratively. Option B: Keep building nodes but build iteratively using an explicit stack. Option C: Keep recursion but ensure depth is bounded (not in your case). Let’s do iterative inorder for your T structure. For a binary tree, standard iterative inorder: def inorder_iter(root): out = [] stack = [] node = root while stack or node: while node: stack.append(node) node = node.left node = stack.pop() out.append(node.val) node = node.right return out But note: your internal nodes have val=None. Leaves have integers. So out will contain many None values. Maybe you intended to only append leaves? If you want leaves only, check node.val is not None. Now, build() is also recursive and will fail at depth 2000. We can build iteratively for your skewed structure because your data is always [left, right] with left being previous nested structure. But for general nested lists, iterative building is trickier. Given make_deep(n) always creates [x, i], you can build without recursion: def build_deep(data): # data is nested [prev, i] # Walk down left links collecting rights, then build back up rights = [] cur = data while isinstance(cur, list): if len(cur) != 2: raise ValueError("Expected pair") rights.append(cur[1]) cur = cur[0] # cur is leaf node = T(cur) for r in rights: node = T(None, node, T(r)) return node This avoids recursion entirely and handles depth 2000+. If you need general build for arbitrary nested pairs, you can still do it iteratively using a stack of frames representing “we’re building this list, next build left/right”. Want the general version?
Yes, general version please. My real data is nested pairs but not always skewed; still can be deep. I’d like an iterative build that handles arbitrary nested pair lists.

Here’s a general iterative build for nested pairs represented as Python lists/tuples of length 2, where atoms become leaves. We’ll simulate recursion with an explicit stack of frames. Approach: depth-first construction. Each frame holds (data, state, left_node). state=0 means left not built yet; state=1 means left built and now building right. Pseudo: - Start with root_data on stack. - While stack not empty: - Look at top frame. - If frame.data is atomic: create leaf node; pop frame; attach to parent if exists. - Else (pair): if state=0: push left child frame elif state=1: push right child frame else: both built -> create internal node and pop. We need a place to store built child nodes. Let’s implement: class Frame: __slots__ = ("data", "state", "left") def __init__(self, data): self.data = data self.state = 0 self.left = None def build_iter(data): stack = [Frame(data)] result = None while stack: top = stack[-1] d = top.data if not isinstance(d, (list, tuple)): node = T(d) stack.pop() if stack: parent = stack[-1] if parent.state == 0: parent.left = node parent.state = 1 elif parent.state == 1: # right child done; create parent node right = node left = parent.left stack.pop() node2 = T(None, left, right) # attach node2 upward if stack: grand = stack[-1] if grand.state == 0: grand.left = node2 grand.state = 1 else: # grand.state == 1 # will handle by setting as right and bubbling similarly # easiest: push a synthetic atom? But we can loop-bubble. # We'll bubble in a loop: carry = node2 while True: if not stack: result = carry break g = stack[-1] if g.state == 0: g.left = carry g.state = 1 break elif g.state == 1: right2 = carry left2 = g.left stack.pop() carry = T(None, left2, right2) continue continue else: result = node2 continue else: result = node continue # It's a pair if len(d) != 2: raise ValueError(f"Expected pair, got len={len(d)}: {d}") if top.state == 0: stack.append(Frame(d[0])) elif top.state == 1: stack.append(Frame(d[1])) else: raise RuntimeError("Invalid state") return result This works but is more complex because of bubbling up when completing a right child; we can simplify by storing both left and right in the frame and only creating the node when both are done, then treat created node as the “result” of that frame to attach to parent. That’s cleaner. Let’s rewrite cleanly: class Frame: __slots__ = ("data", "state", "left", "right") def __init__(self, data): self.data = data self.state = 0 self.left = None self.right = None def build_iter(data): stack = [Frame(data)] last_built = None while stack: f = stack[-1] d = f.data if not isinstance(d, (list, tuple)): last_built = T(d) stack.pop() else: if len(d) != 2: raise ValueError("Expected pair") if f.state == 0: f.state = 1 stack.append(Frame(d[0])) continue if f.state == 1: f.left = last_built f.state = 2 stack.append(Frame(d[1])) continue if f.state == 2: f.right = last_built last_built = T(None, f.left, f.right) stack.pop() continue # Attach last_built to parent frame if needed is handled by reading last_built when parent resumes return last_built This version relies on last_built representing the subtree built from the most recent frame. It should handle deep nesting without recursion. Then you can do inorder_iter to avoid recursion there too. Does that align with your input constraints (pairs only)?

If internal nodes have val=None and leaves have actual values, you can do an iterative traversal that collects only leaves. Two straightforward approaches: 1) Iterative inorder but append only if node.left is None and node.right is None (leaf), or node.val is not None. 2) Use an explicit stack for DFS and push right then left; when at a leaf, append. If you want left-to-right leaf order, inorder-like works. For your binary tree where leaves are atoms, checking leaf-ness is robust: def leaf_values_left_to_right(root): out = [] stack = [] node = root while stack or node: while node: stack.append(node) node = node.left node = stack.pop() if node.left is None and node.right is None: out.append(node.val) node = node.right return out If you want to treat “atom leaf” specifically as node.val is not None (since internal nodes are None), that’s fine too: if node.val is not None: out.append(node.val) But leaf check is safer if None could be a legitimate leaf value. Try that and see if it matches expected output.

Good point about None being a legitimate value. In my case atoms can be None too, so I’ll use leaf check. Another thing: I also have a general graph traversal that sometimes RecursionError’s even with visited. I think I made a mistake with visited being a set of nodes, but my Node defines __eq__. Here’s my Node: class Node: def __init__(self, name): self.name = name self.neighbors = [] def __repr__(self): return f"Node({self.name})" def __eq__(self, other): return isinstance(other, Node) and self.name == other.name Then: def walk(n, visited=None): if visited is None: visited = set() if n in visited: return visited.add(n) for m in n.neighbors: walk(m, visited) I get TypeError: unhashable type: 'Node', so I changed visited to list and now it RecursionError’s sometimes. The TypeError happens because defining __eq__ without defining __hash__ makes instances unhashable (Python sets __hash__ = None to prevent inconsistent hashing). Using a list for visited makes membership checks O(n) and also, if equality is based only on name, you can accidentally treat distinct nodes with same name as same, or vice versa. Options: A) Use identity-based visited: store id(n) in a set: visited_ids = set() if id(n) in visited_ids: return visited_ids.add(id(n)) B) Make Node hashable consistently with __eq__ by defining __hash__: def __hash__(self): return hash(self.name) But careful: if name can change after insertion into a set, that breaks invariants. So name must be immutable. C) Don’t override __eq__ at all; rely on object identity, and use a separate mapping name->node if needed. Given you said you sometimes get RecursionError even with visited list: likely due to equality collisions or because you reset visited incorrectly in recursive calls (not shown), or because the graph is extremely deep (long path > recursion limit). If you do visited as a list of nodes and __eq__ compares by name, then if there are multiple nodes with the same name, you may prematurely mark as visited, but that would reduce recursion, not increase it. RecursionError suggests either (1) visited not working (e.g., using visited=[] default), or (2) depth too deep. What does “sometimes” mean? Can you show a minimal graph that triggers RecursionError with visited as list?

Here’s a reproducer. I create a chain of 3000 nodes: nodes = [Node(str(i)) for i in range(3000)] for i in range(2999): nodes[i].neighbors.append(nodes[i+1]) walk(nodes[0]) That RecursionError’s. With 900 nodes it’s fine.

That’s the deep recursion case again: it’s not a cycle issue; it’s recursion depth. Even with correct visited, a path length of 3000 exceeds default recursion limit (~1000). Fix by using an explicit stack (iterative DFS/BFS) rather than recursion. Iterative DFS: def walk_iter(start): visited = set() stack = [start] while stack: n = stack.pop() if id(n) in visited: continue visited.add(id(n)) # process n here for m in n.neighbors: stack.append(m) If you want deterministic order similar to recursive (preorder), push neighbors in reverse: for m in reversed(n.neighbors): stack.append(m) If you also want to keep __eq__ by name, still use id-based visited to avoid hash issues, or implement __hash__ and store node objects in visited.

I see. If I implement __hash__ using name, is it safe? My nodes’ name is a string and doesn’t change. But I sometimes have two different nodes with same name due to merging from multiple sources. Would that break traversal?

Yes, it changes semantics. If __eq__/__hash__ are based on name, then two distinct nodes with same name will be treated as the same in sets/dicts. That means visited will skip one of them, and your traversal may miss parts of the graph. If you need identity-distinct nodes even if names collide, then don’t define __eq__ by name (or at least don’t use it for visited). A common pattern: - Keep Node equality as identity (default). - If you need “same name” comparisons, compare node.name explicitly when needed. - Use visited as set of node objects (hashable by identity) or ids. If you still want __eq__ by name for other reasons, but want visited by identity, do visited_ids = set() and store id(node). That avoids collisions entirely.

Got it. Another bug: I have a recursive function for evaluating an expression tree that sometimes overflows. I think there’s a cycle but I don’t see it. Here’s a simplified version: class Expr: def __init__(self, op, args): self.op = op self.args = args def eval_expr(e, env): if isinstance(e, (int, float)): return e if isinstance(e, str): return env[e] if e.op == "add": return sum(eval_expr(a, env) for a in e.args) if e.op == "mul": prod = 1 for a in e.args: prod *= eval_expr(a, env) return prod if e.op == "let": name, val_expr, body = e.args env2 = dict(env) env2[name] = eval_expr(val_expr, env2) return eval_expr(body, env2) Sometimes eval_expr hits RecursionError on certain let expressions. However, if val_expr references name (directly or indirectly), you can get infinite recursion or KeyError depending. Example: let x = x + 1 in ... would try to evaluate x using env2 where x not set yet, raising KeyError, not RecursionError. But if env already has x, it would use that, not recurse. RecursionError could happen if your environment contains thunks or expressions that reference themselves, e.g., env["x"] = Expr("add", ["x", 1]) and then evaluating "x" triggers eval_expr on an Expr which includes "x" again, etc. But in your code, env[e] returns whatever is stored; if env stores an Expr, you’ll return Expr, not evaluate it—unless you store expressions and expect them to be evaluated lazily, in which case you need to detect and evaluate, but that can cycle. Also, your "let" uses env2 inside eval_expr(val_expr, env2). If you intended sequential let (where val_expr sees previous env, not including the new binding), you should pass env, not env2. But that wouldn’t create recursion by itself. Can you show an example of env and expression that triggers RecursionError?

In that expression, val_expr references "x" while defining "x". Your evaluator tries to resolve "x" from env2, but env2 doesn't have "x" yet, so env2["x"] should KeyError. But you’re not seeing KeyError because "x" is also used as the body and maybe you have try/except in real code? Or perhaps env is not empty and contains x as an Expr referencing x, causing recursion. In the simplified example as written, env2[name] assignment calls eval_expr on Expr("add", ["x",1]). That evaluates "x" by env["x"] and should raise KeyError. If you want to forbid recursion, detect it: when evaluating let binding, mark variable as “in progress” and error if referenced. Example: IN_PROGRESS = object() def eval_expr(e, env): ... if isinstance(e, str): v = env[e] if v is IN_PROGRESS: raise ValueError(f"Recursive definition of {e}") return v ... if e.op == "let": name, val_expr, body = e.args env2 = dict(env) env2[name] = IN_PROGRESS val = eval_expr(val_expr, env2) env2[name] = val return eval_expr(body, env2) If you want to support recursive lets (like letrec), you need lazy evaluation with memoization and cycle detection. Which behavior do you want: error on recursive definitions, or support them?

You can implement “force” semantics: when a variable resolves to an Expr, evaluate it and memoize the result back into env to avoid repeated evaluation. But you must detect cycles: variable x depends on x (directly or indirectly). Use an evaluation stack or IN_PROGRESS marker per variable. Sketch: IN_PROGRESS = object() def resolve_var(name, env): v = env[name] if v is IN_PROGRESS: raise ValueError(f"Cyclic reference involving {name}") if isinstance(v, Expr): env[name] = IN_PROGRESS res = eval_expr(v, env) env[name] = res return res if callable(v): # decide: call with env? or treat as value? return v return v Then in eval_expr when e is str: return resolve_var(e, env) And in let: env2 = dict(env) env2[name] = IN_PROGRESS val = eval_expr(val_expr, env2) env2[name] = val return eval_expr(body, env2) But note: resolve_var mutates env, so if you copy env for let, memoization stays local to that scope. That may be fine. Also, if env contains Exprs that reference other vars, resolve_var will evaluate them and memoize, but it can still recurse deeply if dependency chain is long; that’s a different recursion-depth issue. If you want to avoid recursion depth entirely, you’d need to evaluate expressions iteratively (like using an explicit stack for AST evaluation). That’s more complex but doable. Mostly cycles. Depth is usually not huge. One more recursion bug: I wrote a function to flatten a nested dict/list structure, but on some inputs it RecursionError’s even though nesting depth is small. I suspect there’s a self-reference in the data structure. How can I detect that?

Self-referential containers can cause infinite recursion even at small “conceptual” depth. Example: a = []; a.append(a). Any recursive walk will loop forever unless you track object identities of containers you’ve already entered. For flattening, track visited container ids. On entering a list/dict/set/tuple, check id(obj) in seen; if so, either skip or raise. Example: def flatten(x, seen=None, path=""): if seen is None: seen = set() if isinstance(x, (dict, list, tuple, set)): oid = id(x) if oid in seen: raise ValueError(f"Cycle detected at {path}") seen.add(oid) if isinstance(x, dict): out = {} for k, v in x.items(): sub = flatten(v, seen, f"{path}.{k}" if path else str(k)) # merge sub with prefix, depending on desired output seen.remove(id(x)) return out elif isinstance(x, list): ... else: return x Important: remove id(x) from seen when leaving if you want to allow the same container to appear in multiple places without it being considered a cycle in that path (though in Python, if the same object is referenced twice but not cyclic, you might still want to treat as aliasing). If your goal is to avoid infinite recursion, tracking “currently on stack” (gray set) is enough; for detecting any shared references, keep a global “seen ever” set. If you provide your flatten function and expected output format, I can adapt it.
