That helps. But I also have tuples and sets in the data. Tuples can be recursive too? And sets contain unhashable stuff? How should I handle those?

Tuples can be recursive via containing a reference to themselves indirectly, but it’s rarer; still possible with objects. Sets can contain only hashable items, so they can’t directly contain a list/dict, but they can contain a custom object that references containers. For flattening, you need to define what to do with sets/tuples: treat them like lists (sequence) with indices, or treat sets as unordered sequences. If you want to flatten tuples like lists, include tuple in the container types and iterate with enumerate. For sets, you either: - Convert to a sorted list of reprs (stable-ish) if elements are comparable, or - Enumerate in arbitrary order (not stable), or - Represent set as a leaf (don’t expand). Because flattening typically wants deterministic keys, sets are awkward. Many implementations treat set as leaf and store it as-is or as list. For cycle detection, you can track ids for any container type you traverse: dict, list, tuple, set. But if you decide sets are leaves, you don’t traverse into them, so no need. If you do traverse into sets, use enumerate(list(s)) and be aware order is arbitrary. Also, if you include tuple, remember to remove id at exit as with list/dict.
Switching gears: I tried to “fix” recursion by using sys.setrecursionlimit(100000). It worked once, but then Python crashed. Why?

sys.setrecursionlimit only changes Python’s recursion check threshold; it doesn’t increase the C call stack size. Very deep Python recursion consumes C stack frames and can segfault the interpreter. So large values can crash instead of raising RecursionError. That’s why the recommended approach is to avoid deep recursion by using iterative algorithms or by restructuring data.
Makes sense. Could you help me convert a recursive postorder traversal of a general tree to iterative? My nodes have variable children list. RecursionError happens on deep trees.

For an N-ary tree postorder (children first, then node), iterative traversal typically uses either: - Two stacks (push node to stack1, pop to stack2; push children to stack1; then pop stack2 to output), or - One stack with a visited flag. Two-stack method: def postorder(root): if root is None: return [] s1 = [root] s2 = [] while s1: n = s1.pop() s2.append(n) for c in n.children: s1.append(c) out = [n.val for n in reversed(s2)] return out But careful: order among siblings depends on push order. If you want left-to-right postorder, push children in order onto s1, but because stack is LIFO, you might need to push them in order and reverse at the end; with the two-stack method, to preserve left-to-right, you usually push children as they appear, and the reversed s2 yields correct postorder for that push order. If it’s off, push children reversed. One-stack with flag: def postorder(root): out = [] stack = [(root, False)] while stack: n, expanded = stack.pop() if n is None: continue if expanded: out.append(n.val) else: stack.append((n, True)) for c in reversed(n.children): stack.append((c, False)) return out This preserves left-to-right if children are in n.children order.

Great. I implemented the one-stack version. Now I’m seeing another issue: my tree building code accidentally creates cycles sometimes, and the iterative traversal loops forever. How do I add cycle detection to the iterative postorder?

For a structure that might have cycles, you need a visited set. For postorder with cycles, you must be careful: a simple visited check can skip nodes prematurely or break postorder semantics. But if your goal is “visit each node once” and avoid infinite loops, track visited identities when first seen. For the one-stack method: def postorder_safe(root): out = [] stack = [(root, False)] seen = set() while stack: n, expanded = stack.pop() if n is None: continue nid = id(n) if expanded: # Only append if we actually expanded it out.append(n.val) continue if nid in seen: continue seen.add(nid) stack.append((n, True)) for c in reversed(n.children): stack.append((c, False)) return out This ensures each node is output once. If you need to detect/report cycles rather than silently skip, you can track “in current path” (gray set) similar to recursion stack. In iterative form, you can manage a state machine: when you push (n, entering), add to gray; when you pop (n, exiting), remove from gray. If you encounter a node already in gray, that’s a cycle. That’s closer to true cycle detection. Example: ENTER, EXIT = 0, 1 def postorder_detect_cycle(root): out = [] stack = [(root, ENTER)] gray = set() black = set() while stack: n, state = stack.pop() if n is None: continue nid = id(n) if state == ENTER: if nid in black: continue if nid in gray: raise ValueError("Cycle detected") gray.add(nid) stack.append((n, EXIT)) for c in reversed(n.children): stack.append((c, ENTER)) else: gray.remove(nid) black.add(nid) out.append(n.val) return out This will raise on cycles and still avoid recursion.
That gray/black idea is helpful. Is this basically the iterative equivalent of DFS with colors?

Exactly. gray = in-progress (on the call stack), black = finished. That’s the standard cycle detection technique for directed graphs (DFS). Using it in iterative traversal mimics recursion stack behavior.
I also have a recursive topological sort that RecursionError’s on big graphs. Could you show an iterative topological sort that also detects cycles?

Use Kahn’s algorithm (BFS on indegrees), which is iterative and naturally detects cycles (if you can’t process all nodes, there’s a cycle). Assuming graph adjacency dict: {node: [neighbors]} Steps: 1) Compute indegree for all nodes. 2) Initialize queue with nodes indegree 0. 3) Pop queue, append to order, decrement indegree of neighbors; if neighbor hits 0, enqueue. 4) If order length < number of nodes => cycle. Implementation: from collections import deque def topo_sort(graph): indeg = {u: 0 for u in graph} for u, nbrs in graph.items(): for v in nbrs: if v not in indeg: indeg[v] = 0 indeg[v] += 1 q = deque([u for u, d in indeg.items() if d == 0]) order = [] while q: u = q.popleft() order.append(u) for v in graph.get(u, []): indeg[v] -= 1 if indeg[v] == 0: q.append(v) if len(order) != len(indeg): raise ValueError("Cycle detected; no topological ordering exists") return order This avoids recursion and handles large graphs well.
Nice. In my case nodes are objects, not hashable sometimes. I can use id-based keys again, but then I lose mapping back to node objects. How do you handle that?

If nodes aren’t hashable, you can still store them in dicts by identity if you make them hashable by identity (default) by not defining __eq__. But if you can’t change the class, you can wrap identity: - Use id(node) as key and keep a separate dict id->node. - Or use weakref.WeakKeyDictionary if objects are hashable by identity (still requires __hash__). If nodes are unhashable due to custom __eq__, id-based is simplest: def topo_sort_nodes(nodes, edges_fn): # nodes: iterable of node objects # edges_fn(u) -> iterable of neighbor node objects id2node = {} indeg = {} adj = {} for u in nodes: uid = id(u) id2node[uid] = u indeg.setdefault(uid, 0) adj.setdefault(uid, []) for v in edges_fn(u): vid = id(v) id2node[vid] = v adj.setdefault(uid, []).append(vid) indeg.setdefault(vid, 0) indeg[vid] += 1 from collections import deque q = deque([uid for uid, d in indeg.items() if d == 0]) order_ids = [] while q: uid = q.popleft() order_ids.append(uid) for vid in adj.get(uid, []): indeg[vid] -= 1 if indeg[vid] == 0: q.append(vid) if len(order_ids) != len(indeg): raise ValueError("Cycle detected") return [id2node[uid] for uid in order_ids] You need a way to enumerate all nodes. If the graph is only reachable from a root, you can first do an iterative reachability walk to gather nodes.

That’s helpful. Another recursion-related issue: I wrote a memoized recursive function for Fibonacci as a toy, but I accidentally got recursion anyway. Here’s the code: cache = {} def fib(n): if n in cache: return cache[n] if n <= 1: return n cache[n] = fib(n-1) + fib(n-2) return cache[n] This works. But when I adapted it to compute something on a graph, it recursed infinitely. I think memoization didn’t help because of cycles. How do you combine memoization with cycle detection?

Memoization prevents repeated work in DAGs; it doesn’t solve cycles unless you detect “in progress” computations. Pattern: - memo dict for completed results - visiting set (or IN_PROGRESS marker) for active recursion path Example generic: IN_PROGRESS = object() memo = {} def f(x): if x in memo: if memo[x] is IN_PROGRESS: raise ValueError("Cycle detected") return memo[x] memo[x] = IN_PROGRESS res = ... f(dep) ... memo[x] = res return res Or separate: memo = {} visiting = set() def f(x): if x in memo: return memo[x] if x in visiting: raise ValueError("Cycle") visiting.add(x) res = ... visiting.remove(x) memo[x] = res return res If x is an unhashable object, use id(x) as key. This is exactly what you want for graph DP or expression evaluation: detect recursion cycles and fail cleanly.
Could you show a concrete example on a graph? Suppose I want to compute “cost” of a node as 1 + sum(cost(children)). If there’s a cycle, error. If it’s a DAG, compute with memoization.

Sure. For node objects with .children list: IN_PROGRESS = object() def cost(node, memo=None): if memo is None: memo = {} nid = id(node) if nid in memo: v = memo[nid] if v is IN_PROGRESS: raise ValueError(f"Cycle detected at {node}") return v memo[nid] = IN_PROGRESS total = 1 for c in node.children: total += cost(c, memo) memo[nid] = total return total This runs in O(V+E) for DAG-like structures reachable from node. If there’s a cycle, you get a ValueError. If you want to return partial results or handle cycles differently, you can.
That answers it. Now, I’m still curious: how can I debug where recursion is looping? Like, print a trace, but without printing thousands of lines. Any tips?

A few practical techniques: 1) Print only when depth is small or at intervals. Pass depth parameter and print when depth % 100 == 0. 2) Use sys.setprofile or sys.settrace to track calls, but that’s heavy. 3) Maintain an explicit stack/path list of nodes/keys; when you detect a cycle (encounter IN_PROGRESS), print the path slice showing the cycle. For example, in the cost() function, keep a stack list and an index mapping from nid->position when first pushed: def cost(node, memo, stack, pos): nid = id(node) if nid in memo: v = memo[nid] if v is IN_PROGRESS: i = pos[nid] cycle = stack[i:] + [node] raise ValueError("Cycle: " + " -> ".join(repr(n) for n in cycle)) return v memo[nid] = IN_PROGRESS pos[nid] = len(stack) stack.append(node) total = 1 for c in node.children: total += cost(c, memo, stack, pos) stack.pop() pos.pop(nid, None) memo[nid] = total return total Call with cost(root, {}, [], {}). That yields a compact cycle description.
That’s very good. I’ll use that. Another question: in Python, is tail recursion optimization a thing? Could it help?

Python does not do tail call optimization. Even tail-recursive functions consume stack frames. So rewriting recursion to tail recursion won’t prevent RecursionError. Use iteration or explicit stack instead.
Understood. I also saw people suggest using functools.lru_cache for memoization. Does it help with recursion depth?

It helps with repeated subproblems (like Fibonacci), but not with recursion depth on a single deep chain. If the recursion depth is N, you still have N nested calls even if caching helps elsewhere. For deep chains, you still need iterative methods or a different algorithm.

I’m going to share a more complex snippet that’s causing RecursionError, and I want help refactoring it iteratively. It’s a recursive descent parser for a simple grammar. The parse() function calls parse_expr which calls parse_term etc. On long inputs it hits recursion limit. How do I handle that?

Recursive descent can hit recursion limit if the grammar is right-recursive or if input causes deep nesting (e.g., many parentheses). For long flat sequences like a+a+a+..., recursion can also happen if implemented recursively per operator rather than loop. For expression parsing, a standard fix is to use loops for left-associative operators, and/or implement a Pratt parser (top-down operator precedence) which is iterative-ish but still recursive for precedence levels; or use shunting-yard algorithm (fully iterative) to convert to RPN then build AST. If you paste your parser code and grammar, I can suggest targeted changes. Common pattern: def parse_expr(): node = parse_term() while peek() in ('+', '-'): op = next() rhs = parse_term() node = BinOp(op, node, rhs) return node This avoids recursion for repeated + operations. Parentheses nesting still can cause deep recursion because parse_factor calls parse_expr inside parentheses. If inputs can have >1000 nested parentheses, recursion will still overflow; then you’d want an iterative algorithm like shunting-yard. Want shunting-yard example?
Yes, shunting-yard would be great. Grammar: numbers, +, *, parentheses. I need an AST. Input can be extremely long and sometimes has many nested parentheses, so recursion is risky.

Shunting-yard converts infix to postfix (RPN) using operator and output stacks, handling parentheses iteratively. Then build AST from RPN using a stack. Operators: + (precedence 1), * (precedence 2), both left-associative. Tokenization: iterate through string and produce numbers and symbols. Implementation outline: 1) tokens = list(tokenize(s)) 2) Convert to RPN: - for token in tokens: - if number: output.append(token) - if op: while ops and ops[-1] is op with >= precedence and left-assoc: output.append(ops.pop()) ops.append(op) - if '(' : ops.append('(') - if ')' : pop until '(' 3) After tokens: pop remaining ops to output 4) Build AST: - for t in output: - if number: push Num(t) - if op: b=pop(); a=pop(); push BinOp(op, a, b) - result = stack.pop() This is iterative and handles arbitrarily deep parentheses. If you want, I can write a complete working snippet with classes.
Please do. Also include unary minus if easy.

Unary minus complicates tokenization and parsing because '-' can be unary or binary. In shunting-yard, you can treat unary minus as a separate operator 'NEG' with higher precedence and right associativity. Rules: when you see '-', it’s unary if it occurs at start or after '(' or another operator. Here’s a full example: class Num: __slots__ = ("value",) def __init__(self, value): self.value = value def __repr__(self): return f"Num({self.value})" class BinOp: __slots__ = ("op","left","right") def __init__(self, op, left, right): self.op=op; self.left=left; self.right=right def __repr__(self): return f"BinOp({self.op}, {self.left}, {self.right})" class UnOp: __slots__ = ("op","expr") def __init__(self, op, expr): self.op=op; self.expr=expr def __repr__(self): return f"UnOp({self.op}, {self.expr})" def tokenize(s): i=0 while i < len(s): c=s[i] if c.isspace(): i+=1; continue if c.isdigit(): j=i while j < len(s) and s[j].isdigit(): j+=1 yield ("NUM", int(s[i:j])) i=j continue if c in "+-*()": yield (c, c) i+=1 continue raise ValueError(f"Unexpected char {c!r} at {i}") OPS = { "+": (1, "L", 2), "-": (1, "L", 2), "*": (2, "L", 2), "NEG": (3, "R", 1), } def to_rpn(tokens): out=[] ops=[] prev_type = None  # None, "NUM", ")", "OP", "(" for typ, val in tokens: if typ == "NUM": out.append(("NUM", val)) prev_type = "NUM" elif typ == "(": ops.append("(") prev_type = "(" elif typ == ")": while ops and ops[-1] != "(": out.append(("OP", ops.pop())) if not ops: raise ValueError("Mismatched ')'") ops.pop() prev_type = ")" else: # operator + - * op = val if op == "-" and (prev_type in (None, "OP", "(")): op = "NEG" # pop by precedence p1, assoc1, _arity = OPS[op] while ops and ops[-1] != "(": op2 = ops[-1] p2, _assoc2, _ = OPS[op2] if (assoc1 == "L" and p1 <= p2) or (assoc1 == "R" and p1 < p2): out.append(("OP", ops.pop())) else: break ops.append(op) prev_type = "OP" while ops: op = ops.pop() if op == "(": raise ValueError("Mismatched '('") out.append(("OP", op)) return out def ast_from_rpn(rpn): st=[] for typ, val in rpn: if typ == "NUM": st.append(Num(val)) else: op = val prec, assoc, arity = OPS[op] if arity == 1: if len(st) < 1: raise ValueError("Insufficient operands") a = st.pop() st.append(UnOp(op, a)) else: if len(st) < 2: raise ValueError("Insufficient operands") b = st.pop() a = st.pop() st.append(BinOp(op, a, b)) if len(st) != 1: raise ValueError("Invalid expression") return st[0] def parse_expr(s): tokens = list(tokenize(s)) rpn = to_rpn(tokens) return ast_from_rpn(rpn) This avoids recursion entirely and handles deep nesting parentheses (limited by memory, not recursion).
I tested it; works. One last recursion error: I wrote a pretty-printer that recursively prints the AST with indentation. Deep trees cause RecursionError again. Any iterative pretty-printer approach?

Yes: use an explicit stack for traversal, emitting “enter” and “exit” events. For a tree, you can do a manual DFS with states. For example, for Num, UnOp, BinOp: def pretty(root): lines=[] stack=[(root, 0, "ENTER")] while stack: node, indent, state = stack.pop() pad = "  " * indent if isinstance(node, Num): lines.append(f"{pad}Num({node.value})") elif isinstance(node, UnOp): if state == "ENTER": lines.append(f"{pad}UnOp({node.op})") # then print child stack.append((node.expr, indent+1, "ENTER")) elif isinstance(node, BinOp): if state == "ENTER": lines.append(f"{pad}BinOp({node.op})") # push right then left so left prints first stack.append((node.right, indent+1, "ENTER")) stack.append((node.left, indent+1, "ENTER")) else: lines.append(f"{pad}{node!r}") return "\n".join(lines) This prints preorder with indentation. If you want parentheses formatting (infix), you can do an in-order traversal with explicit states and add parentheses tokens to a list, again using a stack: def to_infix(root): parts=[] stack=[(root, 0)]  # 0=enter,1=after left,2=after right while stack: node, st = stack.pop() if isinstance(node, Num): parts.append(str(node.value)) elif isinstance(node, UnOp): if st == 0: parts.append("(-") stack.append((node, 1)) stack.append((node.expr, 0)) else: parts.append(")") elif isinstance(node, BinOp): if st == 0: parts.append("(") stack.append((node, 2)) stack.append((node.right, 0)) stack.append((node, 1)) stack.append((node.left, 0)) elif st == 1: parts.append(f" {node.op if node.op!='NEG' else '-'} ") else: parts.append(")") return "".join(parts) That avoids recursion and handles deep trees.
Perfect. I think I’ve got a handle on recursion errors now: cycles vs depth. Thanks for walking through iterative alternatives and cycle detection patterns.

Glad it helped. If you paste any remaining real-world snippet that still triggers RecursionError, the quickest diagnosis is: check for cycles with id-tracking, and if it’s just depth, convert to an explicit stack/queue or a non-recursive algorithm (Kahn, shunting-yard, iterative DFS).
Can you walk me through why Dijkstra's is O((V+E) log V) with a priority queue?

Dijkstra’s algorithm has two main kinds of work: 1) Priority-queue operations (extract-min and decrease-key/insert). 2) Edge relaxations (scanning adjacency lists). Assume a graph with V vertices and E edges, represented with adjacency lists. Core loop idea: - Initialize dist[source] = 0, others = ∞. - Put the source in a min-priority queue keyed by dist. - Repeatedly extract the vertex u with smallest tentative dist. - For each edge (u, v) with weight w, try to relax: if dist[u] + w < dist[v], update dist[v] and update v’s key in the priority queue. Now count operations: A) Extract-min happens once per vertex (in the typical “settled” version). - Each time you pop the smallest vertex u, you permanently finalize dist[u] (because weights are nonnegative). - That’s V extract-min operations. - In a binary heap, extract-min is O(log V), so total O(V log V). B) Relaxations: you examine each edge once (or twice in undirected graphs, depending on representation). - For each edge (u, v), you do a constant-time comparison and possibly a priority-queue key update. - Total edge scans: O(E). C) Priority-queue updates: up to one per relaxation that succeeds. - In a standard implementation with decrease-key, there can be up to E decrease-key operations (one per successful relaxation; in worst case, many relaxations succeed). - Each decrease-key in a binary heap is O(log V). - So decrease-key contributes O(E log V). Combine: - O(V log V) + O(E log V) + O(E) = O((V + E) log V) (since E log V dominates E for V ≥ 2). That’s the classic bound for adjacency list + binary heap.
I get the high-level counting, but I’m fuzzy on why decrease-key is counted as O(E). Isn’t it at most V times, since each vertex’s distance “finalizes” once?

A vertex’s distance finalizes once (when it’s extracted as the current minimum), but its tentative distance can improve multiple times before that finalization. Decrease-key is applied to a vertex whenever you find a better path to it (a successful relaxation). How many successful relaxations can happen overall? - Each edge (u, v) is considered once when u is extracted (in the settled version). - When you look at (u, v), you might improve dist[v]. If so, you perform a decrease-key (or an insert, depending on implementation). - In the worst case, many edges can cause improvements. There’s no rule that “each vertex improves only once.” A vertex v can have its dist decreased multiple times from different incoming edges, as you discover better and better routes. Upper bound: - Each edge can cause at most one successful relaxation at the moment it is examined (it either improves dist[v] or not). - Therefore, the number of successful relaxations is ≤ E. - Each successful relaxation triggers at most one decrease-key (or push). So decrease-key operations are O(E) in the worst case. You might be thinking: “But dist[v] can’t decrease more times than the number of incoming edges to v.” Exactly—and summing indegrees over all vertices gives E.
If I implement Dijkstra without decrease-key, just pushing new (dist, vertex) pairs and ignoring stale entries when popped, does the complexity change?

Yes, the bound changes slightly, depending on how you count heap size and operations, but it stays in the same general family for binary heaps. “Lazy” approach: - Whenever you find a better distance for v, you push a new pair (newDist, v) into the heap. - You do not decrease-key; you just insert. - When you pop (d, v), if d != dist[v], it’s stale and you skip processing v’s edges. Counts: - You still do at most E successful relaxations (each can cause an insert), so up to O(E) inserts. - You also do extract-min potentially more than V times: you might pop many stale entries. How many? At most the number of inserts, i.e. O(E), plus the initial push, etc. So O(E) pops. - Each heap operation is O(log N) where N is heap size. Heap size can grow to O(E) in worst case. So the runtime becomes: - O((#pushes + #pops) log E) = O(E log E) in worst case, plus scanning edges O(E) for the times you settle vertices. But note: with stale skipping, you might still only scan each adjacency list once when you first pop the true best entry for that vertex, so edge scans remain O(E). The dominant factor is heap operations. Since log E is O(log V^2) = O(2 log V) = O(log V) when E is polynomial in V (which it is for simple graphs, E ≤ V^2), people often simplify O(E log E) to O(E log V). But if you want a precise expression: - Using lazy inserts: O((E + V) log E) typically, which is O(E log E). With a proper decrease-key and a heap that supports it efficiently, you get O((V+E) log V) with fewer heap entries.
Can you show a concrete example where a vertex gets decreased many times? Like a small graph that causes multiple updates.

Sure. Consider a graph where many different predecessors offer progressively better paths to the same vertex. Vertices: s, a, b, c, v Edges (directed, weights nonnegative): - s -> a (1) - s -> b (2) - s -> c (3) - a -> v (100) - b -> v (50) - c -> v (10) Run Dijkstra from s: - Start: dist[s]=0. - Relax edges from s: dist[a]=1, dist[b]=2, dist[c]=3. - Extract-min: a (dist 1). Relax a->v: dist[v] becomes 101 (∞ -> 101). Decrease-key/insert v. - Extract-min: b (dist 2). Relax b->v: dist[v] becomes 52 (101 -> 52). Decrease-key/insert v again. - Extract-min: c (dist 3). Relax c->v: dist[v] becomes 13 (52 -> 13). Decrease-key/insert again. Eventually v is extracted with dist 13. Here v’s tentative distance decreased 3 times before finalization. Scale this pattern: if v has indegree k, it can be decreased up to k times. Summing over all vertices gives O(E) possible decreases.
That makes sense. Now, why do we say extract-min is V times? In the lazy version, it could be more.

In the standard “decrease-key” version with a visited/settled set, extract-min that leads to processing adjacency lists happens exactly V times, once per vertex, because: - Each iteration extracts the unsettled vertex with smallest tentative distance. - That vertex is then marked settled and never inserted/updated again (in a strict implementation). So you have V settle events. In a decrease-key implementation, you typically maintain exactly one heap node per unsettled vertex (or at least you conceptually do). Extract-min removes one vertex from the heap permanently. That’s V removals. In the lazy “multiple entries” approach: - You might pop stale entries, so the number of pop operations can be larger, up to O(E). But the number of times you actually finalize/process a vertex is still V. When people quote O((V+E) log V) they’re implicitly using: - A priority queue that supports decrease-key efficiently, - And a representation where each vertex appears once in the queue (or can be updated in place).
I’ve heard Fibonacci heaps give O(E + V log V). Where does that come from?

Same counting framework, but different per-operation costs. With Fibonacci heaps (amortized): - insert: O(1) amortized - decrease-key: O(1) amortized - extract-min: O(log V) amortized Dijkstra does: - V extract-min operations → O(V log V) - Up to E decrease-key operations → O(E) - Some inserts → O(V) typically Total: O(E + V log V) This is asymptotically better than O((V+E) log V) when E is much larger than V (e.g., dense graphs). But Fibonacci heaps are complex and have large constants; in practice, binary heaps often win.
For dense graphs, I’ve also seen O(V^2) for Dijkstra. How does that fit in?

That’s the classic array-based implementation (no heap) and/or adjacency matrix representation. If you implement Dijkstra like this: - Maintain dist[]. - Repeatedly select the unsettled vertex u with minimum dist by scanning all vertices: O(V) per selection. - Do this V times → O(V^2). - Relax outgoing edges. If you use adjacency matrix, scanning all neighbors is O(V) per vertex → another O(V^2). Overall O(V^2). When the graph is dense (E ~ V^2), compare: - Binary heap: O((V + E) log V) ≈ O(V^2 log V) - Array-based: O(V^2) So for dense graphs, O(V^2) is better asymptotically, and often in practice. With adjacency lists, dense graphs still have E = Θ(V^2) edge scans. The heap overhead adds a log V factor. That’s why people sometimes choose the O(V^2) variant for dense graphs.
Can we step back and define Big-O precisely? I feel like I use it but don’t internalize what it means.

Big-O is an asymptotic upper bound on growth rate. Formally, f(n) is O(g(n)) if there exist constants c > 0 and n0 such that for all n ≥ n0: f(n) ≤ c * g(n). Interpretation: - For sufficiently large n, f(n) grows no faster than g(n) up to a constant factor. - Big-O ignores constant multipliers and lower-order terms. Examples: - 3n + 10 is O(n). - n^2 + 100n is O(n^2). - (V+E) log V is O(E log V) if E dominates V, but careful: you can’t drop V in general unless you state assumptions (like E ≥ V). Big-O is an upper bound, not necessarily tight. If you want tight bounds, you use Θ (Theta). For Dijkstra with binary heap under the standard model, the time is Θ((V+E) log V) in worst case (assuming decrease-key is needed and edges cause enough updates).
In that formal definition, what is “n” for graph algorithms? Sometimes it’s V, sometimes it’s E.

Graph algorithms often naturally depend on two parameters, V and E, so the runtime is expressed as a function of both: T(V, E). You can still use Big-O with multiple variables: T(V, E) ∈ O(f(V, E)) means there exist constants c, V0, E0 such that for all V ≥ V0, E ≥ E0: T(V, E) ≤ c f(V, E). Common conventions: - Use adjacency list representation, so scanning neighbors costs proportional to degree, and total neighbor scans is O(E). - Use adjacency matrix, so scanning neighbors is O(V) per vertex, total O(V^2), and E may not appear explicitly. Sometimes people reduce to a single parameter n by assuming a relationship like E ≤ V^2 and then writing bounds in terms of V only. But in interview settings, stating T(V, E) is usually the clearest.
For Dijkstra, is it always correct to say E relaxations? What about directed vs undirected?

With adjacency lists: - Directed graph: each directed edge is stored once; total scans is exactly E when you process each vertex’s outgoing list once. - Undirected graph: typically you store each undirected edge twice (u in v’s list and v in u’s list), so the total adjacency entries is 2E. Complexity becomes O((V + 2E) log V) = O((V+E) log V) since constant factors are ignored. So yes: “O(E)” relaxations means “O(number of adjacency-list entries)”, which is Θ(E) for directed and Θ(2E) for undirected.
I’ve seen variants that stop early when they reach a target node. Does that change worst-case complexity?

Worst-case complexity stays the same, because the worst case could still require exploring almost the whole graph before the target is settled. But best-case or average-case can improve: - If the target is close to the source in terms of shortest-path distance and the graph structure causes it to be extracted early, you might process only a fraction of vertices/edges. In asymptotic worst-case analysis, we assume adversarial inputs, so early stopping doesn’t change the worst-case bound.
How do I reason about complexities for BFS and DFS compared to Dijkstra?

BFS and DFS on adjacency lists are O(V + E). Reason: - Each vertex is enqueued/pushed and visited once: O(V). - Each edge is examined a constant number of times: O(E). No priority queue, so no log factors. Dijkstra is like BFS generalized to weighted graphs (nonnegative weights). The priority queue is what introduces the log factor because you need to repeatedly pick the smallest tentative distance among unsettled vertices.
If weights are only 0 or 1, I’ve heard you can do better than Dijkstra with a deque. What’s the complexity?

That’s 0–1 BFS. - Use a deque instead of a priority queue. - When relaxing an edge of weight 0, push the neighbor to the front; weight 1, push to the back. This maintains nodes in nondecreasing distance order. Complexity: O(V + E), because each vertex/edge is processed a constant number of times and deque operations are O(1). It’s essentially a specialized case where the priority queue can be simulated by a deque due to limited weight values.
What about Dial’s algorithm for small integer weights?

Dial’s algorithm (bucket-based Dijkstra) works when edge weights are nonnegative integers bounded by C. Idea: - Distances are integers. - Maintain an array of buckets (like a circular array) where bucket[i] holds vertices with distance = currentDistance + i. - Extract-min becomes scanning buckets until you find a nonempty one; decrease-key becomes moving a vertex between buckets. Complexity: - O(E + V * C) in a common form, or more precisely O(E + W) where W is the maximum distance value processed, which is O(VC) in worst case. If C is small (constant or small compared to log V), this can outperform binary heap.
Coming back to priority queues: why is extract-min O(log V) in a binary heap?

In a binary heap: - The heap is a complete binary tree stored in an array. - The minimum element is at the root (index 0 or 1). Extract-min steps: 1) Remove the root (min). 2) Move the last element in the array to the root position. 3) “Sift down” (heapify down): compare with children and swap with the smaller child until the heap property is restored. The height of a complete binary tree with n elements is ⌊log2 n⌋. Each sift step moves down one level, and does O(1) comparisons/swaps per level. So extract-min is O(log n). In Dijkstra, n is at most V (with decrease-key version) or up to E (lazy version).
And decrease-key is also O(log V) because it sifts up?

Exactly. Decrease-key steps: 1) Reduce the key value of a node (distance). 2) If the heap property is violated (node smaller than parent), “sift up” by swapping with parent until the heap property holds. Each swap moves up one level; maximum levels is O(log n). So decrease-key is O(log n). One caveat: to do decrease-key efficiently, you need a way to find the heap position (index) of the vertex’s heap node in O(1). That usually means storing a pointer/index per vertex.
That “need index per vertex” part seems important. In code, how do people do that?

Common pattern: - Heap stores items (vertex, key). - Maintain an array pos[vertex] = index in heap array. When you swap heap elements i and j, update pos for both vertices. Then decrease-key for vertex v: - i = pos[v] - heap[i].key = newKey - sift up from i. This yields O(log V) decrease-key. If you don’t maintain pos, you’d have to search the heap to find v, which is O(V), destroying the intended complexity.
So in interview contexts, if I say “use a priority queue” but don’t mention decrease-key details, is that okay?

It depends on how deep they want to go, but conceptually: - Saying “binary heap priority queue; decrease-key is log V” is usually sufficient. - If they ask implementation details, mention either: a) Use a heap with handles/indices (pos array) to support decrease-key, or b) Use the lazy insertion method and accept O(E log E) (often simplified to O(E log V)). Being explicit about which approach you’re using shows you understand where the complexity comes from.
Can we derive the exact expression for Dijkstra with binary heap and decrease-key? Like include all terms.

A more explicit breakdown: Initialization: - dist array init: O(V) - heap build: Option 1: insert V vertices: O(V log V) Option 2: build heap from array of V entries: O(V) heapify Often simplified as O(V) or O(V log V) depending on method. Main loop: - extract-min: V times → O(V log V) - for each edge (u, v): relax check → O(E) - for each successful relax: decrease-key → O(#successes log V) ≤ O(E log V) Total: O(V) + O(heap init) + O(V log V) + O(E) + O(E log V) = O((V + E) log V + E + heap init) Asymptotically: O((V+E) log V). If you want to be pedantic about heap init: - If you use “insert all vertices initially,” that adds O(V log V), still absorbed. - If you use “insert only source then insert neighbors as discovered,” number of inserts could be O(E) in worst case unless you guard against duplicates; typical implementations still keep it O(V) inserts with decrease-key.
Wait, why would inserting only as discovered lead to O(E) inserts? Isn’t each vertex discovered once?

If you do it carefully, each vertex can be inserted once and then updated via decrease-key. But if you don’t have decrease-key or don’t track whether a vertex is already in the heap, you could insert duplicates. Two patterns: 1) Proper decrease-key version: - Insert all vertices once at start (or insert on first discovery). - Keep a boolean inHeap[v] to avoid multiple inserts. - Use decrease-key for improvements. Then inserts are O(V), decreases O(E). 2) Lazy version: - Every improvement triggers a push, even if v is already in heap. Then pushes are O(E). So the “O(E) inserts” is mainly for the lazy approach.
How does this compare to Bellman-Ford complexity-wise?

Bellman-Ford: - Relax all edges repeatedly for V-1 passes. - Each pass scans E edges. Time: O(VE). Space: O(V). It handles negative edge weights (and can detect negative cycles), which Dijkstra cannot. So Dijkstra is much faster on nonnegative graphs: - Dijkstra with heap: O((V+E) log V) - Bellman-Ford: O(VE) On sparse graphs (E ~ V), Dijkstra is about O(V log V) vs Bellman-Ford O(V^2). Big difference.
Could you explain why Dijkstra fails with negative weights, in terms of its “settling” step?

Dijkstra relies on this invariant: When you extract the vertex u with minimum tentative distance, dist[u] is final—no future path can improve it. This is true only if all edge weights are nonnegative. Why? - Any alternate path to u that goes through some other unsettled vertex x would have dist[x] ≥ dist[u] at the time u is extracted (since u is minimum in the queue). - If weights are nonnegative, then any path from x to u adds ≥ 0, so dist[x] + pathWeight ≥ dist[x] ≥ dist[u]. So it can’t beat dist[u]. With a negative edge, you could have: - u extracted with dist[u] = 5 - later discover x with dist[x] = 6 - but there is an edge x -> u with weight -10 Then dist[u] could become -4, contradicting finalization. So the greedy choice “settle the current minimum” breaks.
I want to get better at recognizing when log factors appear. Any general rule of thumb?

Log factors often come from: - Balanced tree operations (BST insert/search/delete): O(log n) - Heap operations (push/pop/decrease-key): O(log n) - Divide-and-conquer recursion depth: O(log n) (like mergesort’s recursion height) - Doubling/halving processes (binary search): O(log n) In graph algorithms: - If you repeatedly need “the smallest element among a changing set,” you usually use a priority queue, which introduces log factors (unless you can bucketize keys, as in 0–1 BFS or Dial’s algorithm). If the algorithm processes each edge/vertex a constant number of times with O(1) data-structure operations, it tends to be linear O(V+E).
Can we talk about MST algorithms too? Like why Prim’s with a heap is similar complexity.

Prim’s algorithm with adjacency lists and a binary heap is very similar: - Maintain a min-heap keyed by the cheapest edge connecting each vertex to the growing MST. - Each extract-min selects the next vertex to add: V times → O(V log V) - Each edge can cause a decrease-key when it improves the best known connection: up to E decreases → O(E log V) So Prim’s is O((V+E) log V) with binary heap, and O(E + V log V) with Fibonacci heap (same pattern as Dijkstra). The difference is what the key means: - Dijkstra key: current shortest distance from source. - Prim key: cheapest edge weight to connect vertex to MST.
Is Kruskal’s complexity also related to sorting edges?

Yes. Kruskal’s: 1) Sort edges by weight: O(E log E) 2) Scan edges, union-find operations: O(E α(V)) ~ almost linear So total: O(E log E), often written O(E log V) because log E = O(log V^2) = O(log V). This again shows a pattern: log factor from sorting, not from a priority queue.
Back to Dijkstra: when people say O((V+E) log V), is that worst-case or average-case?

That’s a worst-case bound under the usual RAM model assumptions and a comparison-based heap. Average-case depends on input distribution and implementation details, but the standard quoted complexity is worst-case. If you want expected bounds: - Some randomized heaps have expected O(log n) operations too, still leading to similar expected complexity. But the canonical statement is worst-case.
Can you do a full “from scratch” walkthrough of Dijkstra complexity, like you’re teaching it to someone who only knows loops and arrays?

Sure, using adjacency lists and a binary heap with decrease-key. Setup: - dist[1..V] array - visited[1..V] boolean - heap of vertices keyed by dist - adjacency list adj[u] contains pairs (v, w) Algorithm structure (conceptually): 1) Initialization: for each vertex v: dist[v] = INF visited[v] = false dist[s] = 0 build a heap containing all vertices with their keys dist[v] (or insert them one by one) Cost: - Setting arrays: O(V) - Building heap: O(V) if heapify, or O(V log V) if repeated insert 2) Main loop: repeat V times: u = extractMin(heap)          // O(log V) if visited[u] continue (optional) visited[u] = true for each (v, w) in adj[u]:    // total over all u is E if not visited[v] and dist[u] + w < dist[v]: dist[v] = dist[u] + w decreaseKey(heap, v, dist[v]) // O(log V) Now count: - The outer loop runs V times because each extractMin removes one vertex from the heap. So extractMin called V times → V * O(log V) = O(V log V). - The inner adjacency loop iterates over each outgoing edge once when its source u is processed. Across all u, total iterations = E. So the “if” check and addition/comparison are O(1) each → O(E). - decreaseKey is called only when relaxation succeeds. How many successes can there be? At most one per edge examination, so ≤ E. Thus total decreaseKey cost ≤ E * O(log V) = O(E log V). Add them up: O(V log V) + O(E) + O(E log V) + initialization = O((V+E) log V).
Sometimes I see O(E log V) instead of O((V+E) log V). Is dropping V log V always safe?

Not always, but often. Since E can be as small as V-1 (a tree) or even 0 in degenerate cases, V log V might not be dominated by E log V. Example: - If E = V-1, then (V+E) log V ≈ (2V) log V = Θ(V log V). - E log V ≈ (V-1) log V = Θ(V log V) too, so it’s fine up to constant factors. But if E is extremely small (say E = 0), then E log V = 0 but you still do V extract-min operations: Θ(V log V). So in a strict sense, O(E log V) alone is not a correct upper bound for all graphs. People write O(E log V) under assumptions like E ≥ V or connected graphs where E ≥ V-1, or they’re using a version that doesn’t extract-min for unreachable vertices (e.g., they stop when heap min is INF). If you implement that optimization, you might extract fewer than V vertices in disconnected graphs. So safest general statement: O((V+E) log V).
That last point is interesting: stopping when min is INF. Does that reduce extract-min count?

Yes, for disconnected graphs when you only care about reachable vertices from the source. If you stop when the smallest dist in the heap is INF, that means remaining vertices are unreachable, and further relaxations won’t happen. Then: - You only extract vertices in the reachable set R. - Extract-min count becomes |R|, and edge scans become edges in the reachable subgraph. So complexity becomes O((|R| + E_R) log V) (or log |R| depending on heap size details), where E_R is number of edges out of reachable vertices. Worst-case still has |R| = V and E_R = E, so asymptotic worst-case unchanged.
Can you compare the complexity of Dijkstra on sparse vs dense graphs with some numbers?

Sure, suppose: - Sparse: E ≈ V - Dense: E ≈ V^2 Binary heap Dijkstra: - Sparse: O((V+E) log V) ≈ O(2V log V) = O(V log V) - Dense: O((V+V^2) log V) ≈ O(V^2 log V) Array-based O(V^2) Dijkstra: - Sparse: O(V^2) (worse than V log V) - Dense: O(V^2) (better than V^2 log V) So: - Sparse graphs: heap-based is best. - Dense graphs: array/matrix-based can be asymptotically better.
I’m also trying to understand the difference between O(log V) and O(log E) in these contexts. When does it matter?

It matters when the heap size is closer to E than V. - In a “proper” decrease-key implementation, the heap stores at most one entry per vertex → heap size O(V) → log factor is log V. - In the lazy implementation, the heap can store one entry per successful relaxation, potentially O(E) entries → log factor is log E. Since E can be much larger than V (up to V^2), log E can be about 2 log V. Asymptotically, that’s still O(log V), but if you’re being precise: - log E = log(V^2) = 2 log V in the worst case for simple graphs. So it’s a constant factor difference in the worst case, but in practice it can still matter. In multigraphs where E can be much larger than V^2, log E could be more substantially larger than log V.
Could you clarify “simple graph” vs “multigraph” and why it affects E bounds?

A simple graph: - No parallel edges between the same pair of vertices. - Usually no self-loops. For a simple directed graph: E ≤ V(V-1) = O(V^2). For a simple undirected graph: E ≤ V(V-1)/2 = O(V^2). A multigraph allows parallel edges, so E can be much larger than V^2. In such cases, log E might not be O(log V). But many algorithm analyses assume simple graphs unless stated otherwise.
If I’m asked complexity of Dijkstra in an interview, what assumptions should I state?

A good clear answer: - “Using adjacency lists and a binary heap priority queue with decrease-key, Dijkstra runs in O((V+E) log V).” Optional clarifications: - “If using Fibonacci heap, it’s O(E + V log V) amortized.” - “If using adjacency matrix / array-based min selection, it’s O(V^2).” - “If I don’t have decrease-key and use lazy inserts, it’s O(E log E) (often simplified to O(E log V)).” Stating representation + priority queue type is key.
Let’s switch to another graph algorithm: what’s the complexity of topological sort?

Topological sort (Kahn’s algorithm or DFS-based) on adjacency lists is O(V + E). Kahn’s algorithm: - Compute indegree of each vertex: O(V + E) - Push all indegree-0 vertices into a queue: O(V) - Pop each vertex once, decrement indegree of its neighbors: total decrements is O(E) So total O(V + E). DFS-based: - DFS visits each vertex/edge once: O(V + E) - Push to stack on exit.
Why is indegree computation O(V+E) and not just O(E)?

Computing indegree typically involves: - Initialize indegree array to 0 for all V vertices: O(V) - Then scan all adjacency lists and for each edge (u,v), do indegree[v]++: O(E) So combined O(V + E). If you already have indegrees stored or you skip initialization due to reuse, you might say O(E) for that step, but the standard from-scratch bound includes O(V) initialization.
How about SCC algorithms like Kosaraju or Tarjan?

Both are O(V + E). Kosaraju: 1) DFS on original graph to compute finishing times: O(V+E) 2) Compute transpose graph: O(V+E) (or O(E) if you build it while reading) 3) DFS in decreasing finish time on transpose: O(V+E) Total O(V+E). Tarjan: - Single DFS with stack bookkeeping; each edge/vertex processed constant times. Total O(V+E).
I notice a theme: lots of graph algorithms are O(V+E) unless they need some ordering structure like a heap or sorting.

Exactly. The moment you need: - global minimum selection repeatedly → heap → log - ordering edges by weight → sorting → log - repeated relaxations across all edges for many rounds → multiply factors like V*E (Bellman-Ford) Many foundational graph traversals and decompositions are linear in the graph size with adjacency lists.
Can we talk about A* search complexity? Does it have a Big-O similar to Dijkstra?

A* uses a priority queue like Dijkstra, so per operation costs are similar, but overall complexity depends heavily on the heuristic and what portion of the graph is explored. In the worst case: - With an admissible but uninformative heuristic (h = 0), A* becomes Dijkstra. So worst-case time is O((V+E) log V) with a binary heap (for reachable region). - With a perfect heuristic, it might expand only nodes on the optimal path, making it much faster in practice. But Big-O worst-case doesn’t capture that benefit; worst-case is still Dijkstra-like.
Could you explain “amortized” in the Fibonacci heap claim?

Amortized analysis spreads the cost of expensive operations over a sequence of operations. In Fibonacci heaps: - decrease-key is O(1) amortized, meaning: If you perform a long sequence of operations, the total time for all decrease-key operations is O(#decrease-key), even though some individual decrease-key might trigger cascading cuts and take more than constant time. - extract-min is O(log n) amortized. So the total runtime over the entire Dijkstra run is bounded by O(E + V log V), even though any single operation might occasionally cost more.
Sometimes I mix up amortized vs average-case. Are they different?

Yes. - Average-case: expectation over a distribution of inputs (or randomness in the algorithm), e.g., “expected runtime over random inputs.” - Amortized: worst-case guarantee over a sequence of operations, independent of input distribution. It says: “For any sequence of operations, total time is bounded, so average per operation is small.” So amortized is a deterministic-style guarantee about sequences, not a probabilistic statement.
Let’s do some practice: Suppose I have a graph with V=1e5 and E=3e5. Roughly how many log factors are we talking about?

log2(V) ≈ log2(100,000) ≈ 16.6, so about 17. For binary heap Dijkstra: - Extract-min: V log V ≈ 1e5 * 17 = 1.7e6 “heap steps” - Decrease-key: up to E log V ≈ 3e5 * 17 = 5.1e6 So on the order of ~7 million logarithmic heap adjustments, plus scanning 3e5 edges and constant overhead. This is a rough mental model; constants and memory locality matter a lot in actual runtime.
If edges are stored in adjacency lists, scanning 3e5 edges is small compared to 7 million heap adjustments.

Often yes, the heap operations dominate for sparse graphs when implemented with comparison-based heaps. That’s one reason specialized approaches (like 0–1 BFS or buckets) can be so effective when weights allow, because they reduce or eliminate the log factor.
Can we discuss why E relaxations are “constant time” each? Doesn’t it include a heap operation sometimes?

Good distinction. The relaxation check itself is constant time: - compute dist[u] + w - compare to dist[v] - if smaller, assign dist[v] = newDist That part is O(1). If the relaxation succeeds, you do a heap update (decrease-key or insert), which is not O(1) in a binary heap; it’s O(log V). So total per edge is: - O(1) for the check always, - plus O(log V) sometimes (if it succeeds). Worst-case, “sometimes” could be often enough (up to E times), giving O(E log V).
Is it possible for every edge relaxation to succeed?

Not literally every edge, because once dist[v] gets very small, many incoming edges won’t improve it. But in worst-case analysis, you can construct graphs where a large fraction of edge relaxations succeed, and across all edges you can have Θ(E) successful relaxations. So the worst-case bound treats successful relaxations as O(E).
How do we show a lower bound that Dijkstra with binary heap is Ω((V+E) log V)? Or is it not tight?

The stated Θ((V+E) log V) is a tight bound for that implementation model in the sense that: - You must at least process all edges in the worst case: Ω(E). - And if your priority queue operations take Ω(log V) per extract-min/decrease-key in the comparison model, and you do Θ(V) extract-min plus Θ(E) decreases in worst case, you get Ω((V+E) log V). But subtlety: - There are alternative priority queues (Fibonacci heap) that reduce decrease-key cost, changing the bound. - There are also lower bounds for shortest paths in various models, but they’re more nuanced. So: for “binary heap with decrease-key,” the Θ bound is appropriate.
When you say “comparison model,” you mean the heap compares keys?

Right. A standard binary heap maintains order via comparisons like (a < b). In that model, many operations require Ω(log n) comparisons in the worst case. If keys have special structure (like small integers), you can use non-comparison-based structures (buckets, radix heaps) to beat log factors.
Radix heaps? How do they affect Dijkstra complexity?

Radix heaps can be used for Dijkstra when edge weights are nonnegative integers (or can be scaled) and distances are monotonic (which they are in Dijkstra: extracted distances are nondecreasing). They achieve: - O((V+E) * log C) or similar bounds depending on the key range, where C relates to maximum distance or maximum edge weight. More precisely, there are variants with O(E + V log U) where U is the maximum distance value. They’re a middle ground between binary heaps and bucket methods.
Okay. Switching gears slightly: can you explain how to compute time complexity from pseudocode systematically?

A systematic approach: 1) Identify loops and how many times they run (in terms of input size). 2) Identify expensive operations inside loops (like heap operations, sorting, hashing). 3) Sum costs across iterations; if nested loops, multiply where appropriate. 4) Use upper bounds for variable iteration counts (e.g., sum of degrees = E). 5) Simplify by keeping dominant terms. For graph algorithms: - Replace “for each neighbor” totals with E using the degree-sum property. - Replace repeated “choose min” with data structure cost times number of selections. Dijkstra example: - Outer loop: runs V times (or |R|). - Inner loop across all iterations: total E. - Heap operations: count them and multiply by log size.
Can you explain the degree-sum property you mentioned?

In a directed graph: - Sum of outdegrees over all vertices equals E (each edge contributes 1 to outdegree of its source). - Sum of indegrees also equals E. In an undirected graph: - Sum of degrees equals 2E (each undirected edge contributes 1 to degree of each endpoint). This property is why scanning adjacency lists across all vertices is O(V + E): you have V lists and total list lengths sum to E (or 2E).
That helps. I want to practice with another algorithm: Floyd-Warshall is O(V^3), right? Why?

Yes, Floyd–Warshall is O(V^3). It uses three nested loops: for k in 1..V: for i in 1..V: for j in 1..V: dist[i][j] = min(dist[i][j], dist[i][k] + dist[k][j]) Each loop runs V times and body is O(1), so total operations ~ V^3. Space is O(V^2) for the distance matrix.
Compare that to running Dijkstra from every vertex.

Running Dijkstra from every vertex (all-pairs shortest paths) on nonnegative graphs: - With binary heap: V * O((V+E) log V) = O(V(V+E) log V) = O((V^2 + VE) log V) - If graph is sparse (E ~ V): O(V^2 log V) - If dense (E ~ V^2): O(V^3 log V) Floyd-Warshall: O(V^3) regardless of E, but requires adjacency matrix and handles negative edges (no negative cycles). So: - Sparse graphs: repeated Dijkstra is usually better. - Dense graphs: Floyd-Warshall can be competitive or better asymptotically (no log factor), but constants and memory can be heavy.
For Johnson’s algorithm, what’s the complexity?

Johnson’s algorithm for all-pairs shortest paths (can handle negative weights but no negative cycles): 1) Add a new source connected to all vertices with 0-weight edges. 2) Run Bellman-Ford once: O(VE) to compute potentials h(v). 3) Reweight edges to remove negatives. 4) Run Dijkstra from each vertex: V times. Total: - Bellman-Ford: O(VE) - Dijkstra part: V * O((V+E) log V) with binary heap = O(V(V+E) log V) So overall: O(VE + V(V+E) log V). For sparse graphs (E ~ V), that’s O(V^2 log V + V^2) ~ O(V^2 log V).
When analyzing complexity, how do we justify ignoring constant factors like 2E vs E?

Big-O collapses constant factors because of the multiplicative constant c in the definition. If f(n) = 2E and g(n) = E, then f(n) ≤ 2 * g(n) for all E, so f is O(g). Similarly, 1000E is O(E). That’s why undirected adjacency lists giving 2E scans is still O(E).
But sometimes constant factors matter in practice.

Definitely. Big-O is about asymptotic growth, not actual runtime. Constant factors, cache behavior, memory allocation patterns, and language overhead can dominate for realistic sizes. That’s why: - Binary heap vs Fibonacci heap: Fibonacci heap has better asymptotic bound but often worse constants. - Adjacency matrix vs list: matrix might be faster for dense graphs due to contiguous memory, even aside from asymptotics. But for reasoning and comparing scalability, Big-O is useful.
Let’s do a more formal proof-style explanation: why does Dijkstra settle vertices in nondecreasing distance order?

Proof sketch (nonnegative weights): Let S be the set of settled vertices (already extracted-min). Invariant: For every u in S, dist[u] is the true shortest-path distance from source s to u. Initially S is empty. dist[s]=0, others ∞. At each step, pick u not in S with minimal dist[u] (extract-min). Claim: dist[u] is optimal. Suppose for contradiction there is a shorter path P from s to u with length L < dist[u]. Consider the first vertex x on P that is not in S; let y be the predecessor of x on P (so y is in S, because x is first not in S). When y was settled, the edge (y, x) would have been relaxed, giving dist[x] ≤ dist[y] + w(y,x). But dist[y] equals the true shortest distance to y by induction hypothesis, and the subpath s→y plus edge y→x is exactly the prefix of P to x, so dist[x] ≤ length(prefix to x) ≤ L < dist[u]. Since dist[u] is minimal among not-in-S vertices, we must have dist[u] ≤ dist[x], contradiction. Thus dist[u] is optimal and can be settled. This also implies settled distances are nondecreasing: each new extracted-min has dist at least as large as previous.
That’s clear. Complexity-wise, does that proof relate to why we only extract-min V times?

Indirectly. The correctness proof justifies that once a vertex is extracted-min, it’s final and won’t need further updates, which supports the algorithm structure where each vertex is settled once. But the V extract-min count is more about the implementation: the heap contains unsettled vertices; each extract-min removes one. Since there are V vertices, you can do at most V such removals that lead to settling.
If I’m using adjacency lists, do I ever scan edges of a vertex more than once?

In the standard settled-vertex implementation: - You scan adj[u] once, at the moment u is settled. - You never revisit u’s outgoing edges because u’s dist is final, and relaxing from u again wouldn’t help (it would produce the same candidate distances). So each adjacency list is scanned once → total O(E) scans. In some buggy or alternative implementations (e.g., if you process a vertex multiple times due to stale heap entries and don’t guard with visited), you could rescan edges, increasing runtime. That’s why in lazy Dijkstra it’s important to check if the popped distance matches dist[u] and skip processing edges if stale.
So the “visited” check is essential to keep edge scans at O(E) in the lazy version?

Yes. Two common guards: - If poppedDist != dist[u], skip (stale entry) or - If visited[u] already true, skip Then each vertex’s adjacency list is processed at most once (when you pop the best entry the first time). Without that guard, you might process the same vertex multiple times, causing edge scans to blow up beyond O(E).
If I use visited, do I still need poppedDist != dist[u]?

Either can work, but they differ slightly: - Using visited[u]: When you pop u the first time, you mark visited[u]=true and process edges. Later stale entries for u get skipped quickly. This works because the first time you pop u, it is the minimum tentative distance for u; with nonnegative weights, that first pop should be the final shortest distance if you’re using a min-heap keyed by distances and only pushing improved distances. In lazy insertion, the first time u is popped is indeed with its smallest key among entries for u, which equals dist[u] at that time, and it should be final. - Using poppedDist != dist[u]: This is a more direct stale detection; you can skip visited entirely, and when you pop a pair (d,u) that isn’t the current best dist[u], you skip processing edges. When you pop (dist[u], u), you process edges. After processing, dist[u] won’t change again, so later stale entries won’t match. Many implementations use the poppedDist check; some use both.
Can you give a concise summary of complexities for common shortest path algorithms?

Sure (adjacency lists unless noted): - BFS (unweighted): O(V + E) - 0–1 BFS (weights 0/1): O(V + E) - Dijkstra + binary heap: O((V + E) log V) - Dijkstra + Fibonacci heap: O(E + V log V) amortized - Bellman-Ford: O(VE) - SPFA (queue-based Bellman-Ford heuristic): worst-case O(VE), often faster on some instances (but no better worst-case guarantee) - Floyd-Warshall (matrix): O(V^3) - Johnson’s (Bellman-Ford + Dijkstra): O(VE + V(V+E) log V)
I’d like to test my understanding with a “spot the complexity” exercise. I’ll describe an algorithm and you tell me its Big-O.
Go ahead—include data structures and graph representation if relevant.
Algorithm: For each vertex u, I run BFS starting from u using adjacency lists and record distances. What’s the total time?
BFS is O(V + E) per run. Running it for each of V starting vertices gives: O(V * (V + E)) = O(V^2 + VE). If the graph is dense (E ~ V^2), that’s O(V^3). If sparse (E ~ V), that’s O(V^2).
Next: For each vertex u, I run Dijkstra with a binary heap.
Multiply by V: O(V * (V + E) log V) = O((V^2 + VE) log V). Sparse (E ~ V): O(V^2 log V) Dense (E ~ V^2): O(V^3 log V)
Next: I have a list of E edges, I sort them, then I do union-find checks. Complexity?
Sorting dominates: - Sort: O(E log E) - Union-find: O(E α(V)) ~ near-linear Total: O(E log E).
Great. One more: I have a graph adjacency matrix, and I do Dijkstra by scanning for min each time. Complexity?
O(V^2): - V times selecting min by scanning V vertices: O(V^2) - plus scanning neighbors in matrix: O(V^2) Still O(V^2).
Coming back to Big-O notation: what about Big-Ω and Big-Θ? Can you define them?

Definitions: - f(n) is Ω(g(n)) if there exist c > 0 and n0 such that for all n ≥ n0: f(n) ≥ c * g(n). This is an asymptotic lower bound. - f(n) is Θ(g(n)) if f(n) is both O(g(n)) and Ω(g(n)). This is a tight asymptotic bound (grows at the same rate up to constant factors). Example: - 3n + 10 is Θ(n). - n log n is Ω(n) and O(n^2), but Θ(n log n) if that’s the tightest. For Dijkstra with binary heap, you can say runtime is Θ((V+E) log V) in worst case for that specific implementation.
Is it correct to say BFS is Θ(V+E) or only O(V+E)?

BFS is Θ(V+E) in the standard adjacency-list model, because: - Upper bound: O(V+E) as argued. - Lower bound: Ω(V+E) in worst case because you may have to visit all vertices and examine all edges to fully traverse the reachable graph (and even to confirm reachability). There are inputs where BFS indeed touches everything. So Θ(V+E) is appropriate.
Sometimes I see people write O(E) for BFS. Is that wrong?

It can be incomplete. BFS does: - O(V) vertex operations (enqueue/dequeue, marking visited). - O(E) edge examinations. If E is at least V (common in connected graphs with average degree ≥ 2), O(E) may dominate and people simplify to O(E). But in graphs with small E (like a forest), V can dominate. More precisely: O(V+E). So “O(E)” isn’t universally correct without assumptions.
For Dijkstra, is there any similar simplification pitfalls?

Yes: - Writing O(E log V) can miss the V log V term in disconnected or very sparse graphs (as discussed). - Writing O(E log E) might be more accurate for lazy PQ, but people simplify to O(E log V) assuming E ≤ V^2. The safe approach is to keep both V and E in the expression unless you state assumptions.
I want to understand the relation between log bases. Does it matter if it’s log2 or ln?

In Big-O, log base doesn’t matter because logs of different bases differ by a constant factor: log_a n = (log_b n) / (log_b a). Since Big-O ignores constant factors, O(log2 n) = O(ln n) = O(log10 n). So you can write log n without specifying base.
Could you explain why heaps are preferred over balanced BSTs for priority queues in Dijkstra?

Both can implement a priority queue. Binary heap: - extract-min: O(log n) - decrease-key: O(log n) - insert: O(log n) Typically good constants, compact array storage. Balanced BST (like red-black tree): - extract-min (find min + delete): O(log n) - decrease-key: usually implemented as delete + insert: O(log n) - insert: O(log n) But higher constants, more pointers, worse cache locality. BST does offer: - ability to iterate in order, or support additional operations like predecessor/successor. But for Dijkstra’s needed ops, heaps are typically faster and simpler.
How about pairing heaps? Do they improve Dijkstra?

Pairing heaps have: - decrease-key: often very fast in practice, with good amortized bounds (though the theoretical bounds are subtle and historically debated). - extract-min: O(log n) amortized. In practice, pairing heaps can outperform Fibonacci heaps while still improving decrease-key costs relative to binary heaps for some workloads. But many standard libraries don’t provide them. For interviews, binary heap and Fibonacci heap are the common reference points.
If I’m coding in a language where the standard priority queue doesn’t support decrease-key, what’s the usual approach?

Use the lazy insertion approach: - Whenever dist[v] improves, push (dist[v], v). - When popping, ignore if dist in entry is not current. Complexity: O(E log E) worst-case (often presented as O(E log V)). Correctness remains. If asked, you can mention that to achieve the canonical O((V+E) log V) you’d implement your own heap with decrease-key support.
I’d like to explore complexity of “bidirectional Dijkstra”. Does it help?

Bidirectional Dijkstra runs two simultaneous Dijkstra searches: one forward from source, one backward from target (on reversed edges), stopping when their search frontiers meet. In practice, it can reduce explored area significantly in some graphs (like road networks), but worst-case complexity is still similar asymptotically: - Each side could still explore most of the graph in worst case. So worst-case remains O((V+E) log V). But for many real-world graphs with good structure, it can be much faster.
How does the stopping condition work in bidirectional Dijkstra?

One common approach: - Maintain distF and distB arrays. - Priority queues pqF and pqB. - Alternate extracting-min from whichever queue has smaller min key. - Keep track of best path length found so far: μ = min over meeting vertices v of distF[v] + distB[v]. - Stop when minKey(pqF) + minKey(pqB) ≥ μ. This ensures you cannot find a shorter path by expanding further, given nonnegative weights.
That’s helpful. Now, can we discuss complexity of building adjacency lists from input?

If input is given as a list of edges: - Initialize an array/list for each vertex: O(V) - For each edge (u,v,w), append to adj[u] (and adj[v] if undirected): O(1) amortized per append. Total O(V + E) time and O(V + E) memory. If you need to sort adjacency lists or remove duplicates, that can add extra factors, but plain construction is linear.
What about memory complexity of Dijkstra?

Using adjacency lists: - Graph storage: O(V + E) - dist array: O(V) - visited array: O(V) - parent array (optional): O(V) - heap: O(V) for decrease-key version, or O(E) for lazy version in worst case So: - With decrease-key: O(V + E) memory overall. - With lazy PQ: still O(V + E) for the graph, but heap can grow to O(E), so auxiliary memory can be O(E) additional (still O(V+E) asymptotically, but larger constant).
If I store parent pointers to reconstruct paths, does that change time complexity?

Not asymptotically. When a relaxation succeeds, you set parent[v] = u, which is O(1). So it’s absorbed into the existing relaxation/decrease-key work. Path reconstruction afterward is O(length of path), at most O(V).
Could you explain the difference between worst-case and amortized memory for lazy PQ?

Lazy PQ can accumulate many stale entries, potentially O(E) heap size in worst case. That’s worst-case memory usage during execution. In many instances, the number of successful relaxations and duplicates is smaller, so average heap size might be much smaller. But asymptotically, you should assume O(E) possible. If you periodically clean the heap (not common), you might control growth, but cleaning itself costs time.
Let’s go deeper into how many decrease-keys happen. Is it exactly E in worst case, or could it be less?

It’s at most E, and in worst case it can be Θ(E), meaning proportional to E. It can be less because: - Many edges won’t produce improvements. - Once a vertex is settled, you don’t decrease-key it anymore (in standard Dijkstra). - Graph structure and weights can limit how often distances improve. But for worst-case analysis, you consider instances where many edges do improve distances before vertices are settled.
Is there a known example where it’s Θ(E)?

You can construct layered graphs where each new settled vertex offers slightly better paths to many others, causing many decreases. One conceptual pattern: - A source connects to many intermediate nodes with increasing distances. - Each intermediate node connects to many target nodes with weights chosen so that each time you settle a new intermediate, it improves distances to many targets. This can make the number of successful relaxations proportional to E.
I think I’m getting it. Could you summarize Dijkstra complexities for different PQs and graph representations in a small table-like description (in text)?

Dijkstra (nonnegative weights): Adjacency list: - Binary heap with decrease-key: O((V+E) log V) - Binary heap lazy inserts (no decrease-key): O(E log E) (often simplified to O(E log V)) - Fibonacci heap: O(E + V log V) amortized - Bucket/Dial (integer weights ≤ C): O(E + VC) Adjacency matrix: - Array-based min selection: O(V^2)
Nice. One last question: why do we say “log V” rather than “log (V+E)” for heap operations?

Because the heap size in the canonical implementation is tied to the number of vertices in the queue, which is O(V), not O(V+E). In decrease-key Dijkstra, the heap contains at most one entry per vertex, so heap size n ≤ V. Thus operation cost is O(log V). If you used a different structure where heap size could vary, you could write log n, but substituting n ≤ V gives log V. In lazy Dijkstra, heap size can be O(E), so the log factor is log E.
Thanks. Could you end by giving me a checklist I can use when asked to analyze a graph algorithm’s complexity?

Checklist: 1) Identify graph representation: adjacency list (V+E) scanning) vs adjacency matrix (V^2 scanning). 2) Identify core traversal: does it visit each vertex/edge a constant number of times? If yes, expect O(V+E). 3) Look for global ordering needs: - “Pick smallest next” → priority queue → add log factor (or buckets if keys are small integers). - “Process edges by weight” → sorting → E log E. 4) Count how many times each expensive operation occurs: - extract-min: often V times - decrease-key/insert: often up to E times 5) Multiply counts by per-operation cost. 6) Add terms and simplify, keeping both V and E unless assumptions justify dropping one. 7) State assumptions explicitly (nonnegative weights, connectedness, simple graph, etc.).
